d.u: Emperor An of Jin, Sima Dezong

v.t: Tao Yuanming resigns from office and retires to countryside, founding the pastoral poetry school
v.s: Tao Yuanming, a poet of the Eastern Jin, unwilling to 'bow for five dou of rice', resigned as magistrate of Pengze County and completely retired to his countryside. He subsequently created numerous poems such as 'Returning to Live in the Country' and 'Drinking Wine', depicting natural scenery and farming life, expressing his weariness of officialdom and love for simple living. He founded the pastoral poetry school in Chinese literary history, deeply influencing later literati.

v.t: Tufa Lilugong of Southern Liang proclaims himself king, changes era name to Jianhe
v.s: Tufa Lilugong, ruler of Southern Liang, formally proclaimed himself king in the Hexi region and changed the era name to Jianhe. He continued the policies of his elder brother Tufa Wugu, allying with Later Qin against Northern Liang and Western Qin, attempting to consolidate Southern Liang's rule in the Huangshui valley. This political move intensified the power struggles among the various Liang states in the Hexi Corridor.

v.t: Visigothic King Alaric I invades northern Italy for the first time
v.s: Visigothic King Alaric I, dissatisfied with the Western Roman Empire's failure to grant him a high military rank and territory, led his army across the Alps into northern Italy. He captured cities including Aquileia, threatened Milan, and forced Western Roman Emperor Honorius to move his capital from Milan to the more defensible Ravenna. This marked the beginning of a series of Visigothic invasions of Italy.

v.t: Kumarajiva organizes large-scale translation of scriptures in Chang'an, systematically introducing Mahayana Buddhism into central China
v.s: Yao Xing, ruler of Later Qin, revered Buddhism and invited the eminent monk Kumarajiva to stay at Xiaoyao Garden and Ximing Pavilion in Chang'an, where he presided over an unprecedented translation project. Leading a team of over eight hundred monks, Kumarajiva systematically translated important Mahayana scriptures such as the Diamond Sutra, Lotus Sutra, Amitabha Sutra, and Middle Treatise, correcting many errors in earlier translations. This allowed Mahayana Buddhist teachings to be introduced completely and accurately into central China for the first time, profoundly influencing the formation of various Chinese Buddhist schools.

v.t: Maya city-state of Palenque enters a period of rapid development
v.s: The important Maya city-state of Palenque in Mesoamerica began large-scale construction, building complex palace and temple complexes. The city later became an artistic and cultural center of the western Maya region, with its exquisite reliefs and inscriptions representing the highest artistic achievements of the Maya Classic period.

d.u: Emperor An of Jin, Sima Dezong

v.t: Sun En's rebellion fails, he drowns himself
v.s: Sun En, leader of the Way of the Five Pecks of Rice rebellion in Eastern Jin, suffered continuous defeats from Liu Yu, a general of the Northern Army, near Linhai. His forces were devastated, and with nowhere to turn, Sun En drowned himself along with several hundred followers. The rebellion had lasted three years, affecting eight commanderies including Kuaiji and Wu, severely weakening the rule of the aristocratic clans and providing Liu Yu with military and political capital for his rise.

v.t: Roman general Stilicho defeats Alaric at the Battle of Pollentia
v.s: Stilicho, the de facto ruler of the Western Roman Empire and a general of Vandal descent, engaged Visigothic King Alaric in a decisive battle at Pollentia (near modern Piacenza, Italy). Using the discipline and tactical advantages of the Roman legions, Stilicho repelled the Visigothic cavalry and forced Alaric to withdraw. Though the Visigoths suffered heavy losses, their main force remained intact, and the two sides entered negotiations. The battle temporarily defended the Italian heartland.

v.t: Huan Xuan enters Jiankang and takes control of the court
v.s: Huan Xuan, Inspector of Jingzhou in the Eastern Jin, exploited internal court conflicts to lead his army down the Yangtze and enter the capital Jiankang (modern Nanjing). He killed the regents Sima Daizi and Sima Yuanxian, seized control of the court, and appointed himself Prime Minister, Grand Commandant, and Governor of Yangzhou, monopolizing military and political power. His dictatorship paved the way for his usurpation the following year and further disintegrated the central authority of the Eastern Jin.

v.t: Visigothic King Alaric I issues the Breviary of Alaric
v.s: To effectively rule both the Visigoths and Roman subjects within Roman territory, Visigothic King Alaric I, with the assistance of Roman jurists, issued the Breviary of Alaric (Breviarium Alaricianum). Primarily adapted from the Roman Theodosian Code and incorporating traditional Visigothic customary laws, it became the earliest written code of the Visigothic Kingdom and significantly influenced the development of medieval European law.

v.t: Western Roman Empire formally moves capital to Ravenna
v.s: Due to the growing threat of Visigothic invasion, Western Roman Emperor Honorius, on the advice of General Stilicho, formally moved the capital of the Western Roman Empire from Milan to Ravenna on the Adriatic coast. Surrounded by marshes and easily defensible, Ravenna also had an excellent port for communication with the Eastern Roman Empire. Ravenna remained the political center of the Western Roman Empire for its last seventy-plus years.

v.t: Christian monasticism flourishes in the eastern Roman Empire
v.s: Under the influence of Church Fathers such as Saint Pachomius and Saint Basil, communal monasticism spread rapidly in Asia Minor, Syria, and Egypt within the Eastern Roman Empire. Monasteries became centers of learning and charity, attracting many to abandon secular life. This movement laid the foundation for Western Christian monasticism, with the Rule of Saint Benedict gradually taking shape in this context.

d.u: Emperor An of Jin, Sima Dezong

v.t: Huan Xuan forces Emperor An of Jin to abdicate, establishes Huan Chu regime
v.s: After a year in control of the Eastern Jin court, Huan Xuan forced Emperor An, Sima Dezong, to abdicate and proclaimed himself emperor at Jiankang, naming his state Chu (known as Huan Chu). Huan Xuan, however, was dissolute, extravagant, and cruel, losing popular support. Remaining Eastern Jin loyalists and regional inspectors rose up against him, and the Huan Chu regime faced severe internal and external crises from the start.

v.t: Roman general Stilicho defeats Alaric again at Verona
v.s: Visigothic King Alaric again invaded northern Italy, and Roman general Stilicho engaged him near Verona. Using terrain advantages and elite Roman cavalry, Stilicho defeated the Visigothic army. Alaric was forced to abandon his plan to attack Ravenna and withdrew his remaining forces from Italy. This was Stilicho's second major victory over the Visigoths, temporarily stabilizing the Western Roman situation.

v.t: Faxian arrives in India, makes pilgrimage to Buddhist holy sites
v.s: The Eastern Jin monk Faxian, after enduring the perils of deserts and snowy mountains, arrived in India (Tianzhu). He made pilgrimages to holy sites such as Shravasti in Kosala, Kapilavastu, Bodh Gaya, and Sarnath, and studied Sanskrit and Buddhist scriptures at a Mahayana monastery in Pataliputra, Magadha. Faxian brought back many Vinaya texts, becoming the first Chinese monk to successfully travel by land to India and return with scriptures.

d.u: Emperor An of Jin, Sima Dezong

v.t: Liu Yu raises troops at Jingkou to attack Huan Xuan
v.s: Liu Yu, a general of the Northern Army of Eastern Jin, gathered over 1,700 veterans at Jingkou (modern Zhenjiang, Jiangsu) and formally rose against the usurper Huan Xuan. He issued a manifesto calling for rebellion, which was answered by regional inspectors, and quickly marched into Jiankang. Huan Xuan's forces suffered repeated defeats, forcing him to flee west to Jingzhou with Emperor An. Liu Yu gradually became the most powerful general in the Eastern Jin.

v.t: Huan Xuan defeated and killed at Jiangling
v.s: After fleeing to Jiangling (modern Jingzhou, Hubei) with Emperor An, Huan Xuan attempted to regroup. Liu Yu sent his subordinates in pursuit. Huan Xuan was again decisively defeated in battle outside Jiangling and was killed by Feng Qian, a protector of Yizhou, while fleeing. Liu Yu welcomed Emperor An back to Jiankang to restore his position, completely quelling the Huan Xuan rebellion. The Eastern Jin imperial house lost all real power, which fell entirely into Liu Yu's hands.

v.t: Hun king Uldin leads invasion of Eastern Roman Thrace
v.s: The Hun chieftain Uldin led a large force of cavalry across the Danube to invade the Eastern Roman province of Thrace, plundering several towns. Eastern Roman Emperor Arcadius was forced to pay a huge ransom and concede Hun control over certain areas along the Danube. Uldin's invasion marked the beginning of direct Hun threats to the Eastern Roman Empire, foreshadowing the large-scale invasions of Attila's era.

v.t: Kumarajiva translates the Great Perfection of Wisdom Treatise
v.s: The Later Qin eminent monk Kumarajiva translated the Great Perfection of Wisdom Treatise (Mahaprajnaparamita Shastra) in Chang'an. This work, a detailed commentary on the Large Perfection of Wisdom Sutra in one hundred rolls, systematically expounded the core ideas of the Madhyamaka school. Its translation greatly promoted the development of Prajna studies in Chinese Buddhism and became an important theoretical basis for the Sanlun and Tiantai schools.

d.u: Emperor An of Jin, Sima Dezong

v.t: Emperor An of Jin restored; Liu Yu controls Eastern Jin government
v.s: After quelling the Huan Xuan rebellion, Liu Yu welcomed Emperor An Sima Dezong back to Jiankang and changed the era name to Yixi. Liu Yu appointed himself Attendant, General of Chariots and Cavalry, and Commander-in-Chief of military affairs, controlling the Eastern Jin's military and political power. He promoted many veterans of the Northern Army and lower-level gentry, began to reform internal affairs, and weakened the power of aristocratic clans, laying the foundation for his later northern expeditions.

v.t: Qiao Zong secedes in Shu, proclaims himself King of Chengdu
v.s: Mao Qu, Inspector of Yizhou, was killed by his subordinates during the campaign against Huan Xuan. The staff officer Qiao Zong took control of Chengdu, proclaimed himself King of Chengdu, and established the Western Shu regime, breaking away from Eastern Jin. Qiao Zong submitted to Western Qin and Later Qin, using their support to maintain his secession for nearly a decade, cutting off Eastern Jin's communication with the southern regions.

v.t: Faxian sets sail from India to return home
v.s: After six years of pilgrimage and study in India, the Eastern Jin monk Faxian boarded a merchant ship at Tamralipti (modern Tamluk, India) to return home. He sailed along the maritime route, crossing the Bay of Bengal to Sri Lanka (then called Lion Kingdom), where he continued studying Buddhist scriptures. Faxian chose the sea route to avoid the turmoil along the overland Silk Road, and his journey left precious records of Sino-Indian maritime traffic.

v.t: Emperor Daowu of Northern Wei implements policy of scattering tribes
v.s: To strengthen centralization, Emperor Daowu Tuoba Gui of Northern Wei implemented a policy of 'scattering the tribes,' forcibly breaking up tribal populations and incorporating them into the emperor's directly controlled 'guoren' system and prefectural-county system. This policy weakened the power of Xianbei tribal nobles and transformed Northern Wei from a tribal confederation into a feudal centralized state, though it also provoked rebellions from some tribal chieftains.

d.u: Emperor An of Jin, Sima Dezong

v.t: Liu Yu implements Tunduan, reorganizes household registration and taxes
v.s: The Eastern Jin regent Liu Yu carried out a nationwide 'Yixi Tunduan' (land and household registration reform), abolishing émigré commanderies and prefectures, registering southern refugee families into local rolls, and levying taxes uniformly. This measure struck at the aristocratic clans who had hidden households, increased state revenue, and eased tensions between northern and southern commoners, accumulating economic strength for Liu Yu's northern expeditions.

v.t: Vandals, Suebi, and Alans cross the Rhine, invade Gaul
v.s: Driven by Hun pressure from the east, the Vandals, Suebi, and Alans jointly crossed the frozen Rhine on December 31, 406, and poured into the Western Roman province of Gaul. The barbarian armies sacked Mainz, Trier, and other cities, completely collapsing the Roman defensive system in Gaul. This was the largest barbarian invasion since the Gauls' incursion in 390 BC, marking the permanent end of Western Roman control over the Rhine frontier.

v.t: Roman troops in Britain proclaim Constantine III emperor
v.s: Fearing barbarian invasion of Britain, the Roman garrison there proclaimed a common soldier, Constantine, as emperor (known as Constantine III). He soon led his forces across the Channel into Gaul and established his rule in Gaul and Spain, rivaling Western Roman Emperor Honorius. Constantine's usurpation further weakened the Western Roman Empire's control over its western provinces.

v.t: Northern Liang ruler Juqu Mengxun captures Dunhuang
v.s: Juqu Mengxun, ruler of Northern Liang, led his army to attack Dunhuang, which was under Western Liang control. After months of siege, the Northern Liang forces captured Dunhuang, forcing Western Liang king Li Gao to move his capital to Jiuquan. The fall of Dunhuang gave Northern Liang control over the western Hexi Corridor, laying the groundwork for its eventual unification of the Hexi region.

d.u: Emperor An of Jin, Sima Dezong

v.t: Helian Bobo proclaims himself Heavenly King of Xia, establishes the Xia regime
v.s: Helian Bobo, chieftain of the Tiefu匈奴 (Xiongnu), proclaimed himself Heavenly King of Great Xia and Great Chanyu, establishing the Xia regime. Based at Tongwan City (modern Jingbian, Shaanxi), he used guerrilla tactics to harass the borders of Later Qin, defeating Southern Liang and Later Qin among others, gradually controlling the Hetao and northern Shaanxi regions. Helian Bobo was known for his cruelty, leaving nothing alive in his path, but he also built the extremely strong capital of Tongwan City.

v.t: Western Roman Emperor Honorius orders withdrawal from Britain
v.s: Unable to handle barbarian invasions in Gaul and Italy, Western Roman Emperor Honorius ordered the last Roman field army in Britain to withdraw to defend Italy. After the garrison left, the Romanized cities of Britain lost military protection and came under increasing attacks by Picts, Scots, and Anglo-Saxons. Roman civilization in Britain rapidly declined.

v.t: Liu Yu sends envoy to Later Qin to request twelve-tone pitch pipes
v.s: Using the pretext of a court ceremony, the Eastern Jin regent Liu Yu sent an envoy to Later Qin to request the twelve-tone pitch pipes that had been passed down from the Former Qin period. Later Qin emperor Yao Xing, out of respect for Eastern Jin culture, agreed to give them. This cultural diplomatic event demonstrated Liu Yu's tough stance in foreign relations and reflected that despite military confrontation between the Northern and Southern dynasties, cultural exchanges continued.

d.u: Emperor An of Jin, Sima Dezong

v.t: Liu Yu begins planning northern expedition against Southern Yan, prepares army
v.s: After several years of internal reforms, the Eastern Jin regent Liu Yu had greatly increased the state's power and military strength. He decided to launch a northern expedition against the Southern Yan regime in Shandong to recover lost territory and build prestige. Liu Yu mass-trained infantry and navy in the capital Jiankang, built warships, gathered intelligence, and prepared for the expedition, also sending envoys to Later Qin and Northern Wei to diplomatically isolate Southern Yan.

v.t: Western Roman Emperor Honorius executes general Stilicho
v.s: A palace conspiracy in the Western Roman Empire led by the minister Olympius falsely accused General Stilicho of plotting to place his son on the throne and secretly colluding with Visigothic King Alaric. The gullible Emperor Honorius ordered Stilicho's execution and the slaughter of many barbarian soldiers loyal to him. This foolish decision cost the Western Roman Empire its best military commander, and many barbarian troops defected to Alaric's camp, severely weakening Roman military strength.

v.t: Faxian arrives in Sri Lanka and stays two years to study Buddhism
v.s: The Eastern Jin monk Faxian arrived by ship from India in Sri Lanka (then called the Lion Kingdom), an important center of Theravada Buddhism. He stayed there for two years, studying the Vinaya and Sutra Pitakas, and copied scriptures including the Mahisasaka Vinaya and the Dirgha Agama. He witnessed the grand tooth relic festival. His stay in Sri Lanka supplemented Buddhist texts he had not been able to obtain in India.

d.u: Emperor An of Jin, Sima Dezong

v.t: Liu Yu leads northern expedition against Southern Yan, reaches Langya
v.s: Liu Yu, Grand Commandant of Eastern Jin, set out from Jiankang with tens of thousands of infantry and cavalry on his northern expedition against Southern Yan. The army advanced via the Huai and Si Rivers to Langya Commandery (modern Linyi, Shandong) in the south of Southern Yan. Liu Yu invented the 'que crescent formation,' using war chariots, shields, and crossbows to create a mobile defensive line that effectively countered Southern Yan cavalry charges. The Jin army's morale was high, laying the foundation for capturing the Southern Yan capital, Guanggu.

v.t: Vandals, Alans, and Suebi cross from Gaul into Spain
v.s: After three years of raiding Gaul, the Vandals, Alans, and Suebi crossed the Pyrenees into the Western Roman province of Hispania. They quickly occupied most of the Iberian Peninsula except for Carthaginiensis, leaving Roman rule in Spain in name only. These barbarians settled in various parts of Spain, setting the stage for the later Visigothic conquest.

v.t: Eastern Jin Inspector of Jingzhou Liu Yi annexes Jiangzhou, intensifying conflict with Liu Yu
v.s: Liu Yi, an Eastern Jin general who had rendered great service in suppressing the Huan Xuan rebellion, was appointed Inspector of Jingzhou. Growing envious of Liu Yu's power, he arbitrarily annexed parts of Jiangzhou and placed his confidants in important positions. The political conflict between Liu Yu and Liu Yi gradually became open, setting the stage for their later power struggle.

d.u: Emperor An of Jin, Sima Dezong

v.t: Liu Yu captures Guanggu, destroys Southern Yan
v.s: Liu Yu, Grand Commandant of Eastern Jin, besieged the Southern Yan capital Guanggu (modern Qingzhou, Shandong). Southern Yan emperor Murong Chao refused to surrender. The Jin army used tunneling, trebuchets, and fire attacks, and after eight months finally breached the city. Liu Yu ordered a massacre. Murong Chao was taken back to Jiankang and beheaded, ending Southern Yan. With this victory, Liu Yu recovered Shandong and the Huaihei region, and his prestige reached its peak.

v.t: Lu Xun and Xu Daofu lead rebel forces northward, approaching Jiankang
v.s: Lu Xun and Xu Daofu, remnants of Sun En's rebellion, regrouped in Guangzhou and Shixing and led their forces in two prongs down the Yangtze. Claiming a hundred thousand men, they advanced by land and water, capturing Changsha, Jiangling, and other major cities, with their vanguard reaching the Qinhuai River mouth near Jiankang. Panic spread in the capital, and the Eastern Jin court was in crisis. Liu Yu rushed his main expeditionary force back to defend Jiankang.

v.t: Visigothic King Alaric sacks Rome
v.s: After negotiations with the Western Roman Empire broke down, Visigothic King Alaric besieged Rome. Barbarian slaves inside opened the Salarian Gate to let the Visigoths in. This was the first time in eight hundred years that the city of Rome had fallen to an external enemy since the Gauls in 390 BC. The Visigoths looted the city for three days, seizing enormous treasure and destroying some buildings, but they did not burn the whole city nor damage the churches. The fall of Rome shocked the entire ancient world; St. Jerome wept bitterly in Bethlehem, and St. Augustine began writing 'The City of God' in response.

v.t: Faxian leaves Sri Lanka by ship, reaches Yavadvipa
v.s: After completing his studies in Sri Lanka, the Eastern Jin monk Faxian boarded a large merchant vessel carrying over two hundred people to return east, laden with many scriptures and Buddhist images. A storm blew the ship off course; after ninety days of drifting, he reached a country called Yavadvipa (generally identified as Java or Sumatra in modern Indonesia). Faxian stayed there for five months, waiting for the monsoons to continue his journey home.

d.u: Emperor An of Jin, Sima Dezong

v.t: Liu Yu defeats Lu Xun outside Jiankang, recovers Jiangzhou
v.s: Liu Yu, Grand Commandant of Eastern Jin, set up defenses outside Jiankang, using fire attacks and elite naval forces to defeat the rebel army of Lu Xun and Xu Daofu. The rebels suffered heavy losses and were forced to retreat west along the Yangtze. Liu Yu pursued and recaptured Jiangzhou (modern Jiujiang, Jiangxi) and parts of Jingzhou. Lu Xun retreated to Guangzhou, and the rebel offensive collapsed.

v.t: Visigothic king Athaulf succeeds Alaric, leads forces to southern Gaul
v.s: After Alaric died soon after the sack of Rome, his brother-in-law Athaulf was chosen as the new Visigothic king. Athaulf changed Alaric's strategy, abandoning plans to expand into Africa and leading the Visigoths out of Italy into southern Gaul (modern Aquitaine, France). The Visigoths established a stable kingdom centered on Toulouse, signing a treaty with the Western Roman Empire as allies (foederati).

v.t: Lu Xun is defeated and drowns himself in Guangzhou
v.s: The Eastern Jin general Du Huidu led forces into the rebels' last stronghold, Guangzhou. Lu Xun organized a desperate resistance but was outnumbered. With no way out, Lu Xun followed Sun En's example and drowned himself. The thirteen-year-long Sun En and Lu Xun rebellion thus ended completely. The rebellion had severely damaged the rule of the Eastern Jin aristocratic clans and accelerated Liu Yu's eventual replacement of the Jin dynasty.

d.u: Emperor An of Jin, Sima Dezong

v.t: Liu Yu tricks and kills Liu Yi, eliminating a political rival
v.s: Liu Yu, Grand Commandant of Eastern Jin, used the pretext that Liu Yi was plotting rebellion in Jingzhou to lead a major western expedition. Liu Yu first sent envoys to appease Liu Yi, lulling him into complacency, then launched a surprise attack. Liu Yi fled to Jiangling but ran out of supplies and reinforcements, eventually hanging himself. Liu Yu absorbed Liu Yi's army and territory, removing his greatest internal political rival and becoming the most powerful man in the court.

v.t: Faxian returns to China by sea with many Buddhist scriptures
v.s: After resting several months in Yavadvipa, the Eastern Jin monk Faxian again boarded a merchant ship. Another storm hit, and the ship eventually landed near Laoshan in Changguang Commandery, Qingzhou (modern Qingdao, Shandong). Faxian's journey had lasted thirteen years, covering over thirty countries. He brought back eleven Sanskrit Buddhist texts, including the Mahasanghika Vinaya and the Mahayana Mahaparinirvana Sutra, making an irreplaceable contribution to the development of Buddhist precepts in China.

v.t: Female philosopher Hypatia murdered by a mob in Alexandria
v.s: Hypatia, the renowned female philosopher, mathematician, and astronomer of Alexandria, was seen as a symbol of secular learning due to her influence. Supporters of Bishop Cyril of Alexandria accused her of persuading the Roman prefect to refuse reconciliation with the bishop. A mob dragged her from her carriage, skinned her with pottery shards, and burned her remains. This event marked an escalation of conflict between classical scholarship and radical Christian forces, leading many scholars to leave Alexandria.

d.u: Emperor An of Jin, Sima Dezong

v.t: Liu Yu orders Zhu Lingshi to pacify Sichuan, destroying Qiao Zong
v.s: Liu Yu, Grand Commandant of Eastern Jin, sent General Zhu Lingshi with twenty thousand troops on a western expedition to crush Qiao Zong's secessionist regime in Sichuan. The Jin army used a surprise strategy, attacking Chengdu from multiple directions. Qiao Zong's forces collapsed at the first encounter, and Qiao Zong hanged himself. Zhu Lingshi captured Chengdu, ending the Western Shu regime and restoring Eastern Jin control over Sichuan.

v.t: Helian Bobo begins construction of Tongwan City
v.s: Helian Bobo, king of Great Xia, conscripted a hundred thousand laborers and craftsmen to build his capital, Tongwan City, on the edge of the desert in modern Jingbian County, Shaanxi. The walls were built using rammed earth steamed with a special technique, making them exceptionally strong; legend has it that if an awl could penetrate an inch into the wall, the builder would be executed. Tongwan City was one of the largest and strongest capitals of the Sixteen Kingdoms period, symbolizing the militaristic nature of the Xia regime and Helian Bobo's cruel rule.

v.t: Visigothic Kingdom and Western Roman Empire sign formal treaty of alliance
v.s: Visigothic king Wallia (who succeeded Athaulf) signed a treaty with Western Roman Emperor Honorius. The Visigoths, as allies (foederati) of Rome, were allowed to settle in Aquitaine in southwestern Gaul, from Toulouse to Bordeaux. They promised to provide troops for the Roman Empire to fight other barbarians, especially the Vandals and Alans. The treaty gave the Visigothic Kingdom legal status and provided an example of the Roman strategy of using barbarians against barbarians.

v.t: Construction of the Theodosian Walls in Constantinople
v.s: Eastern Roman Emperor Theodosius II ordered the construction of new walls for Constantinople, known as the Theodosian Walls. The walls featured a double layer with an outer moat, standing about 12 meters high and stretching about 5.6 kilometers. They were among the most formidable fortifications of the ancient world. No army ever breached Constantinople from the landward side for over a thousand years thereafter, until the Ottomans broke through with cannons in 1453.

d.u: Emperor An of Jin, Sima Dezong

v.t: Faxian arrives in Jiankang, translates Buddhist scriptures at Daochang Temple
v.s: After landing in Qingzhou, the Eastern Jin monk Faxian finally returned to the capital Jiankang, where he was warmly welcomed by the Buddhist community. He stayed at Daochang Temple (modern Douchang Temple in Nanjing) and, together with the Indian monk Buddhabhadra, began translating the Buddhist texts he had brought back, including the Mahasanghika Vinaya and the Mahayana Mahaparinirvana Sutra. Faxian also wrote an account of his journey, the 'Record of Buddhist Countries,' which became an important historical and geographical source for ancient Central and South Asia.

v.t: Visigothic king Wallia decisively defeats Vandals and Alans
v.s: Visigothic king Wallia, fulfilling his treaty with the Western Roman Empire, led his army against the Vandals and Alans who had settled in Spain. The Visigoths routed the Vandal-Alan forces near Cartagena, forcing them to retreat south. The Western Roman Empire thus regained control over parts of Spain, though its strength was insufficient for long-term rule.

v.t: Later Qin emperor Yao Xing attacks Xia, is defeated and retreats
v.s: Yao Xing, emperor of Later Qin, personally led an army to attack Helian Bobo's Xia regime, attempting to recover lost territory in northern Shaanxi. Helian Bobo used a scorched-earth and deep-luring tactic. The Later Qin army, suffering supply difficulties and low morale in the desert, was forced to retreat. The Xia cavalry counterattacked, inflicting heavy losses. This battle further weakened Later Qin and solidified Xia control over the Hetao region.

d.u: Emperor An of Jin, Sima Dezong

v.t: Liu Yu attacks Sima Xiuzhi, eliminates last opposition
v.s: Liu Yu, Grand Commandant of Eastern Jin, used the pretext that Sima Xiuzhi, Inspector of Jingzhou, harbored 'secret plots' to launch a large western campaign. Sima Xiuzhi, a member of the Eastern Jin imperial clan, controlled six commanderies of Yongzhou and Jingzhou and was Liu Yu's last major political rival. The Jin army defeated Sima Xiuzhi's forces, and Sima Xiuzhi fled to Later Qin. Liu Yu absorbed Jing and Yong provinces, leaving no force in Eastern Jin capable of challenging him, bringing him one step away from usurpation.

v.t: Visigothic king Wallia decisively defeats Vandal king Gunderic
v.s: Visigothic king Wallia launched a decisive attack on the Vandals in southern Spain. Vandal king Gunderic was defeated and forced to abandon Spain, leading his people across the sea into North Africa. The Visigoths took control of most of the Iberian Peninsula, establishing the Visigothic-Spanish Kingdom, which became the dominant power in the region after Rome.

v.t: Northern Liang's Juqu Mengxun captures Jiuquan, destroys Western Liang
v.s: Juqu Mengxun, ruler of Northern Liang, besieged the Western Liang capital Jiuquan. Western Liang king Li Xin sallied out to fight, was defeated and killed, and the city fell. Remaining Western Liang forces retreated to Dunhuang, soon also absorbed by Northern Liang. Juqu Mengxun unified the Hexi Corridor, becoming the most powerful force in the northwest, setting the stage for later Northern Wei attacks on Northern Liang.

d.u: Emperor An of Jin, Sima Dezong

v.t: Later Qin emperor Yao Xing dies; sons fall into internal strife
v.s: Yao Xing, emperor of Later Qin, died of illness. His crown prince Yao Hong succeeded. However, other sons Yao Bi and Yao Yin refused to accept this, launching mutinies to fight for the throne. Although Yao Hong suppressed the rebellions, Later Qin's power was severely weakened by the internal strife, army morale was low, and regional generals began to secede. When Liu Yu, Grand Commandant of Eastern Jin, heard the news, he decided to take the opportunity to launch a northern expedition against Later Qin.

v.t: Liu Yu launches massive expedition against Later Qin, dividing into five routes
v.s: Liu Yu, Grand Commandant of Eastern Jin, personally led a large army from Jiankang on a northern expedition against Later Qin. He sent generals Wang Zhen'e and Tan Daoji with five separate routes, advancing by land and water, aiming to recover Guanzhong and destroy Later Qin. The Jin army was highly motivated and widely acclaimed, receiving support from many quarters. This was the largest northern expedition launched by the Eastern Jin since its founding.

v.t: Visigothic king Wallia dies; Theodoric I succeeds
v.s: After Wallia consolidated the Visigothic kingdom, he died and was succeeded by Theodoric I. Theodoric continued to expand the Visigothic sphere of influence, strengthening control over southern Gaul and Spain. During his reign, the Visigothic Kingdom gradually transformed from a barbarian military confederation into a true kingdom, accelerating Roman-Visigothic cultural fusion.

d.u: Emperor An of Jin, Sima Dezong

v.t: Liu Yu's expeditionary force captures Chang'an, destroys Later Qin
v.s: The Eastern Jin expeditionary force broke through Later Qin's defenses. General Wang Zhen'e led a naval force west along the Yellow and Wei Rivers, breaching Tong Pass, while Tan Daoji's land force captured Luoyang. The two armies converged on Chang'an. Later Qin emperor Yao Hong surrendered, and Later Qin was destroyed. Liu Yu recovered the Guanzhong region, lost for nearly a century. The Jin army seized great quantities of treasures, books, and musical instruments, greatly enhancing its prestige.

v.t: Liu Yu forced to return south due to rumors of coup in Jiankang, loses Guanzhong
v.s: Just after capturing Chang'an, Liu Yu received news of the death of his trusted aide Liu Muzhi in Jiankang. Fearing unrest in the court, he left his young son Liu Yizhen and a few generals to defend Guanzhong while he hurried back south with his main force. No sooner had Liu Yu left than Helian Bobo, king of Xia, launched a major attack on Guanzhong. The remaining Jin generals fell into infighting, leading to the loss of Guanzhong, and Liu Yu's gains from capturing Chang'an were quickly lost.

v.t: Liu Yu enfeoffed as Duke of Song, granted nine bestowments
v.s: The Eastern Jin court, in recognition of Liu Yu's achievements in the northern expedition, issued an edict enfeoffing him as Duke of Song and granting him the nine bestowments (jiuxi). This was a traditional prelude to a powerful minister's usurpation. Liu Yu feigned refusal three times before accepting, marking the final stage of his replacement of the Eastern Jin and establishment of a new dynasty.

d.u: Emperor An of Jin, Sima Dezong

v.t: Xia king Helian Bobo captures Chang'an, proclaims himself emperor
v.s: Taking advantage of infighting among the Eastern Jin garrison, Xia king Helian Bobo personally led a large army to besiege Chang'an. The Jin generals in Chang'an—Shen Tianzi, Wang Xiu, Fu Hongzhi—mutually suspected and killed each other, causing the army's morale to collapse. Helian Bobo captured Chang'an, and his army plundered the city mercilessly. He proclaimed himself emperor in Chang'an, changing the era name to Changwu. Xia became the new overlord of Guanzhong and Hetao.

v.t: Liu Yu sends envoys to Later Qin remnants and Liangzhou to request classics and musical instruments
v.s: Liu Yu, Duke of Song, sent envoys to the remnants of Later Qin and various regimes in the Hexi Corridor to request scattered Chinese classic texts, ritual objects, and astronomical instruments. These cultural trophies were shipped back to Jiankang, enriching the Eastern Jin court's collection and providing ritual legitimacy for Liu Yu's establishment of a new dynasty. This act demonstrated Liu Yu's emphasis on Han culture and his desire to position himself as the orthodox inheritor of the Central Plains.

v.t: Bahram V becomes king of the Sassanian Empire
v.s: Sassanian king Yazdegerd I died, and his son Bahram V returned from Hira with support from Arab tribes to ascend the throne at Ctesiphon. Bahram V, famous for hunting wild asses and legendary tales, suppressed a noble rebellion, reestablished Sassanian control over Armenia, and maintained a relatively peaceful relationship with the Eastern Roman Empire.

d.u: Emperor An of Jin, Sima Dezong

v.t: Liu Yu sends Wang Shaozhi to strangle Emperor An of Jin, places Sima Dewen on throne
v.s: Anxious to ascend the throne, Liu Yu, Duke of Song, ordered the Palace Attendant Wang Shaozhi to strangle Emperor An Sima Dezong while he was drunk. Liu Yu then installed the emperor's brother Sima Dewen as emperor, known as Emperor Gong of Jin. Emperor Gong was a complete puppet, and there was no further obstacle to Liu Yu's usurpation.

v.t: Vandal king Gaiseric signs treaty with Western Roman Empire
v.s: After establishing a foothold in North Africa, Vandal king Gaiseric signed a treaty with Western Roman Emperor Honorius. The Western Roman Empire recognized Vandal rule over parts of North Africa, while the Vandals acknowledged the nominal suzerainty of the Western Roman emperor. The treaty bought time for Gaiseric to build strength and eventually capture Carthage.

v.t: Translation of the Mahaparinirvana Sutra begins in Northern Liang
v.s: The Northern Liang king Juqu Mengxun, a devout Buddhist, organized the translation of the Mahaparinirvana Sutra in the capital Guzang (modern Wuwei, Gansu) by the eminent monk Dharmakṣema. The sutra expounded important teachings such as 'all sentient beings have Buddha-nature' and 'nirvana is eternal, blissful, self, and pure,' profoundly influencing the Tiantai, Huayan, and Chan schools of Chinese Buddhism.

d.u: Emperor Wu of Song, Liu Yu

v.t: Liu Yu accepts abdication from Emperor Gong of Jin, establishes Liu Song dynasty
v.s: Liu Yu, Duke of Song, forced Emperor Gong of Jin, Sima Dewen, to abdicate and proclaimed himself emperor at the southern suburb of Jiankang, with the dynasty name Song, changing the era name to Yongchu. Liu Yu was a military general of humble origins. His ascension marked the end of aristocratic clan politics in the Eastern Jin and the beginning of the Northern and Southern Dynasties period. The establishment of Liu Song reestablished the dominance of military men of humble origin in politics.

v.t: Liu Yu implements Tunduan and Dudian, curbs powerful clans
v.s: After ascending the throne, Emperor Wu of Song (Liu Yu) continued the Tunduan policy, strictly auditing hidden households and land. He ordered the abolition of émigré commanderies and prefectures, registering the émigrés into local rolls, while also implementing Dudian (land measurement), recording hidden households and land for taxation. These measures struck at the economic privileges of aristocratic clans, increased state revenue, but also intensified the conflict between the Liu Song and the aristocratic clans.

v.t: Vandal king Gaiseric occupies western Sicily
v.s: Taking advantage of the weakness of the Western Roman Empire, Vandal king Gaiseric sent a fleet from North Africa to capture ports such as Lilybaeum in western Sicily. The Vandals used this as a base to establish maritime hegemony in the western Mediterranean and began raiding the coasts of Italy and Greece. The Western Roman navy was powerless to counter and could only acquiesce.

v.t: Indian mathematicians invent the symbol for zero and decimal place-value system
v.s: Mathematicians in the Gupta period invented the symbol (a circle) for 'zero' and perfected the decimal place-value system. This was a revolutionary breakthrough in mathematical history, making calculations much simpler and more efficient. The Hindu-Arabic numeral system was later transmitted to Europe via Arab merchants, becoming the universal counting system worldwide, profoundly affecting the development of mathematics, science, and commerce.

d.u: Emperor Wu of Song, Liu Yu

v.t: Liu Yu kills Emperor Gong of Jin, ends any possibility of Jin restoration
v.s: Fearing that the deposed Emperor Gong of Jin, Sima Dewen, might be used by Eastern Jin loyalists to restore the dynasty, Liu Yu ordered him suffocated with a quilt. Liu Yu thus established the evil precedent of killing an abdicated monarch; in subsequent dynasties of the Southern Dynasties, most deposed emperors met the same fate. This event marked the increasing brutality of Southern Dynasties politics.

v.t: Last Roman legion in Britain withdraws
v.s: Due to the critical situation in Gaul and Italy, Western Roman Emperor Honorius ordered the last Roman field legion in Britain to withdraw to Gaul. The Romanized cities of Britain lost military protection, and local aristocrats began to organize their own defenses, while Anglo-Saxon incursions became more frequent. The Roman era in Britain formally ended, and the region gradually entered the Anglo-Saxon period.

v.t: Liu Song expeditionary force eliminates Northern Liang remnants in Hexi, recovers some territory
v.s: Emperor Wu of Song (Liu Yu) sent an expedition west to attack the eastern part of the Hexi Corridor controlled by Northern Liang. The Liu Song army captured important military strongholds such as Chouchi and Wudu, and for a time recovered parts of Longyou. However, because Northern Liang still had significant strength and the Liu Song main force needed to guard against Northern Wei, the two sides eventually reached a stalemate. Though this northern expedition achieved limited results, it demonstrated Liu Song's ambition to restore the territory of the Han dynasty.

d.u: Emperor Wu of Song, Liu Yu
Emperor Shao of Song, Liu Yifu

v.t: Emperor Wu of Song dies; Emperor Shao of Song ascends
v.s: Liu Yu, founding emperor of the Liu Song dynasty, died of illness in Jiankang at age 60. His crown prince Liu Yifu ascended the throne, known as Emperor Shao of Song, only 17 years old. On his deathbed, Liu Yu appointed four regents: Xu Xianzhi, Fu Liang, Xie Hui, and Tan Daoji. Emperor Shao was dissolute and ignored state affairs, laying the seeds for his subsequent deposition by the regents.

v.t: Emperor Mingyuan of Northern Wei dies; Emperor Taiwu ascends
v.s: Emperor Mingyuan of Northern Wei, Tuoba Si, died of illness, and his 15-year-old crown prince Tuoba Tao ascended the throne, known as Emperor Taiwu. Emperor Taiwu was one of the most outstanding military commanders in Chinese history. During his reign, he defeated Rouran in the north, destroyed Xia, Northern Liang, and Northern Yan in the west and east, unifying northern China and ending the Sixteen Kingdoms period. His ascension marked the beginning of the rise of Northern Wei.

v.t: Large-scale conflict between Liu Song and Northern Wei along the Yellow River
v.s: Taking advantage of Liu Yu's recent death and the instability of the Liu Song court, Emperor Taiwu of Northern Wei launched a major southern campaign, attacking Liu Song's territories south of the Yellow River. The Northern Wei army captured Huatai, Luoyang, and other cities, while Liu Song defenders resisted stubbornly. The decades-long war between the Liu Song and Northern Wei thus began.

d.u: Emperor Shao of Song, Liu Yifu
Emperor Wen of Song, Liu Yilong

v.t: Xu Xianzhi etc. depose Emperor Shao of Song, install Emperor Wen of Song
v.s: The regents Xu Xianzhi, Fu Liang, and Xie Hui, claiming that Emperor Shao of Song (Liu Yifu) was dissolute and immoral, launched a coup to depose him and soon had him killed. They installed Liu Yu's third son, the Prince of Yidu, Liu Yilong, as emperor (Emperor Wen of Song). After ascending, Emperor Wen outwardly appeased the regents while secretly planning to eliminate them.

v.t: Hun kings Attila and Bleda jointly succeed to the throne
v.s: After the death of Hun king Rugila, his nephews Attila and Bleda jointly succeeded as the rulers of the Huns. Attila was about 17 years old but had already shown exceptional military talent and political ambition. Under their joint leadership, the Hun Empire expanded further, posing an increasing threat to the Eastern Roman Empire.

v.t: Northern Wei captures Hulao Pass; Liu Song northern defenses collapse
v.s: Emperor Taiwu of Northern Wei led his army in a fierce assault on the strategic fortress of Hulao Pass (in modern Xingyang, Henan) on Liu Song's Yellow River defense line. Liu Song commander Mao Dezhu held out for over two hundred days before running out of supplies and being captured. With the fall of Hulao Pass, Liu Song's defenses in Henan completely collapsed, and they retreated to the Huai River line.

d.u: Emperor Wen of Song, Liu Yilong

v.t: Emperor Wen of Song executes Xu Xianzhi and Fu Liang, eliminates the regents
v.s: After consolidating his position, Emperor Wen of Song issued an edict proclaiming that Xu Xianzhi, Fu Liang, and Xie Hui had committed the crime of murdering the young emperor, and sent troops to arrest them. Xu Xianzhi committed suicide, Fu Liang was executed, and Xie Hui rebelled but was also killed. Emperor Wen thoroughly purged the regents, taking full power into his own hands and removing obstacles to the Yuanjia Era of good governance.

v.t: Emperor Taiwu of Northern Wei personally campaigns against Rouran, defeats Chile
v.s: Emperor Taiwu of Northern Wei led a large army north deep into the desert to attack the Rouran Khaganate and the Chile (Gaoche) tribes. The Wei forces heavily defeated the Rouran army at the Ruo River (modern Mongolia), and the Rouran khagan Datan fled far away. Taiwu had an inscription carved on a cliff to commemorate the victory. This campaign greatly weakened Rouran and consolidated Northern Wei's northern border.

v.t: Eastern Roman Empire and Sassanian Persia engage in border war
v.s: The Eastern Roman Empire and the Sassanian Persian Empire again went to war over control of Armenia. Eastern Roman Emperor Theodosius II sent troops to attack the Persian border, and both sides had some victories. The war lasted several years, ending with a treaty that divided Armenia between the two empires: the Eastern Romans received western Armenia, and Persia received eastern Armenia.

d.u: Emperor Wen of Song, Liu Yilong

v.t: Emperor Wen of Song promotes Confucianism, establishes four academies
v.s: Emperor Wen of Song issued an edict to establish four academies in the capital Jiankang: Confucian, Metaphysical (Xuanxue), Historical, and Literary, inviting renowned scholars from across the land to lecture. This was the first time in Chinese history that the government established comprehensive academic institutions, breaking the tradition since Han of teaching only the Confucian classics in the Imperial University, and promoting the development of new learning since the Wei-Jin period, deeply influencing later educational systems.

v.t: Theodosian Walls of Constantinople completed
v.s: The land walls of Constantinople, ordered by Eastern Roman Emperor Theodosius II, were formally completed. The walls consisted of an inner wall, an outer wall, and a moat, standing about 12 meters high and stretching about 5.6 kilometers, making them one of the most formidable defensive systems of the ancient world. For over a thousand years thereafter, Constantinople relied on these walls to withstand sieges by Avars, Arabs, Rus, and Bulgars.

v.t: Vandal king Gaiseric builds a large navy
v.s: To control the Mediterranean, Vandal king Gaiseric built a large fleet of warships in North African ports, creating a highly effective navy. The Vandal navy employed light, fast vessels and excelled in surprise attacks and boarding actions. This fleet later became the maritime hegemon of the western Mediterranean, repeatedly raiding the coasts of Rome and Greece and sacking Rome itself in 455.

d.u: Emperor Wen of Song, Liu Yilong

v.t: Emperor Taiwu of Northern Wei captures Chang'an, Xia forces withdraw from Guanzhong
v.s: Taking advantage of internal strife in Xia, Emperor Taiwu of Northern Wei personally led a large western expedition, capturing Chang'an (modern Xi'an). Xia emperor Helian Chang (son of Helian Bobo) fled to Shanggui (modern Tianshui, Gansu), and Northern Wei took control of the Guanzhong plain. From then on, the Xia regime declined, eventually being destroyed by Northern Wei in 431.

v.t: Nestorian Christian controversy intensifies, leading to religious conflict
v.s: Nestorius, Patriarch of Constantinople, argued that Christ's divine and human natures were separate, objecting to calling the Virgin Mary 'Theotokos' (God-bearer) and preferring 'Christotokos' (Christ-bearer). This position was strongly opposed by Cyril, Patriarch of Alexandria, leading to fierce theological debate. Eastern Roman Emperor Theodosius II tried to mediate, but the conflict grew, setting the stage for the Council of Ephesus in 431.

v.t: Tao Yuanming dies of illness
v.s: Tao Yuanming, the renowned Eastern Jin poet and founder of the pastoral poetry school, died of illness in Xunyang Chaisang (modern Jiujiang, Jiangxi), at about 61 years of age. Tao Yuanming had entered official service five times and retired five times, finally retiring completely to the countryside. His works 'The Peach Blossom Spring,' 'Returning Home,' 'Drinking Wine,' and 'The Biography of Master Five Willows' depicted an ideal utopia and a contented rural life, profoundly influencing later Chinese literature and the culture of reclusion.

d.u: Emperor Wen of Song, Liu Yilong

v.t: Northern Wei captures Tongwan City, destroys main Xia army
v.s: Emperor Taiwu of Northern Wei personally led thirty thousand cavalry in a lightning strike on the Xia capital Tongwan City (modern Jingbian, Shaanxi). The Wei cavalry attacked suddenly; Xia emperor Helian Chang fled in panic, and Tongwan fell. Northern Wei seized vast treasures, horses, and people, and the main Xia army was shattered. Helian Chang was later captured, and his brother Helian Ding succeeded, but the Xia regime lingered on.

v.t: Vandal king Gaiseric captures Carthage
v.s: Taking advantage of political instability in North Africa, Vandal king Gaiseric led his army to capture Carthage (modern Tunis), the capital of Roman North Africa. The Roman garrison offered little resistance. Gaiseric made Carthage the capital of the Vandal Kingdom and established a maritime hegemony in the western Mediterranean. The Roman Empire lost its North African breadbasket, dealing a fatal blow to its economy.

v.t: Juqu Mengxun of Northern Liang sends envoys to pay tribute to Liu Song
v.s: To counter pressure from Northern Wei, Juqu Mengxun, king of Northern Liang, sent envoys south of the Yangtze to submit to Liu Song as a vassal. Emperor Wen of Liu Song awarded Juqu Mengxun the title King of Hexi. This diplomatic gesture won Northern Liang support from the Southern Dynasties, but the geographical distance limited its practical significance.

d.u: Emperor Wen of Song, Liu Yilong

v.t: Yuanjia administration reaches its peak; political stability achieved
v.s: After eliminating the regents, Emperor Wen of Song implemented policies of light taxation, rest with the people, and greatly developed agriculture, handicrafts, and commerce. Within Liu Song, society was stable, household numbers greatly increased, and granaries were full. Literature, history, metaphysics, and Confucianism all flourished, known as the 'Yuanjia Administration.' This was the political, economic, and cultural heyday of the Southern Dynasties for 170 years.

v.t: Council of Ephesus is held; Nestorianism condemned as heresy
v.s: Eastern Roman Emperor Theodosius II convened the third ecumenical council at Ephesus to address Nestorius's teachings. The council, presided over by Cyril of Alexandria, declared Nestorianism heretical and exiled Nestorius. Nestorian Christians later spread eastward into Persia, India, and China (as Jingjiao or 'Luminous Religion'), having a significant impact on the development of Christianity in the East.

v.t: Last Xia emperor Helian Ding captured by Western Qin
v.s: After suffering repeated defeats by Northern Wei, Helian Ding, the last Xia emperor, led his remaining forces west in an attempt to conquer Northern Liang. On the way, he launched a surprise attack on Western Qin, destroying it. But Western Qin remnants allied with Tuyuhun to defeat the Xia army at the Yellow River, and Helian Ding was captured by Tuyuhun. The Xia regime was destroyed. Helian Ding was sent to Northern Wei and executed.

d.u: Emperor Wen of Song, Liu Yilong

v.t: Emperor Taiwu of Northern Wei launches second major campaign against Rouran, carves inscription
v.s: Emperor Taiwu of Northern Wei personally led sixty thousand cavalry north again against the Rouran. The Wei forces advanced light and fast, penetrating over 3,000 li into the desert, breaking the main Rouran army. Rouran khagan Datan fled with his remnants, and hundreds of thousands surrendered. Taiwu had an inscription carved at the site, proclaiming 'No royal court south of the Gobi.' The Rouran never recovered, and the northern frontier of Northern Wei was secure for a long time.

v.t: Vandal king Gaiseric conquers all of North Africa
v.s: Taking advantage of the Roman Empire's inability to reinforce, Vandal king Gaiseric swept across the remaining Roman forces in North Africa, occupying Numidia, Byzacena, and other provinces. The Vandal Kingdom now controlled the North African Mediterranean coast from the Atlantic to Carthage, cutting off the Roman Empire's most important grain supply route. The Roman Empire had to rely on Egypt and Sicily for grain.

v.t: Border conflict between Liu Song and Northern Wei in the Huai River region
v.s: Emperor Wen of Song sent General Dao Yanzhi north on a campaign to recover lost territory north of the Huai. The Liu Song army temporarily recaptured Huatai, Luoyang, and other cities, but Emperor Taiwu of Northern Wei quickly counterattacked. The Liu Song forces were not accustomed to northern cavalry warfare and suffered repeated defeats; Dao Yanzhi fled south. The failure of this northern expedition crushed Liu Song's confidence to contend with Northern Wei.

d.u: Emperor Wen of Song, Liu Yilong

v.t: Liu Song northern expedition fails; all territory south of the Yellow River lost
v.s: After the failure of Dao Yanzhi's northern expedition, Emperor Taiwu of Northern Wei personally led a large army south in pursuit. Liu Song's defenses in Henan collapsed completely; Huatai, Hulao, Luoyang, Jinyong, and other cities fell one after another. Liu Song casualties were heavy, and they retreated south of the Huai River. After this battle, the border between Liu Song and Northern Wei stabilized along the Huai line, and Liu Song completely lost hope of recovering the central plains.

v.t: Augustine of Hippo dies during Vandal siege of Hippo Regius
v.s: Vandal king Gaiseric besieged the North African city of Hippo Regius (modern Annaba, Algeria). The elderly bishop of Hippo, the Christian theologian Augustine, fell seriously ill during the siege and died on August 28 at age 76. Augustine wrote great works such as 'Confessions,' 'The City of God,' and 'On the Trinity,' systematically expounding Christian theology. His influence on medieval and even all of Western Christian thought is immeasurable. Hippo fell soon after his death.

v.t: Irish monks begin large-scale copying of Latin manuscripts
v.s: While the European continent was in chaos, Irish monasteries preserved many Latin biblical and classical texts. Irish monks established scriptoria in their monasteries and mass-copied the Bible, patristic works, and a few classical Latin literary works. This preservation work made Ireland the 'Island of Scholars' in the early Middle Ages, and later Irish missionaries helped rekindle Christianity on the continent.

d.u: Emperor Wen of Song, Liu Yilong

v.t: Vandal king Gaiseric captures Hippo Regius, burns churches
v.s: After a siege of fourteen months, the Vandal army finally captured the North African city of Hippo Regius (modern Annaba, Algeria). The Vandals adhered to Arian Christianity and viewed Catholicism as heretical. Upon entering the city, they extensively destroyed Catholic churches and killed priests who refused to convert. Much of Augustine's writings and his library were also destroyed by the Vandals.

v.t: Huns (Attila and Bleda) invade the Eastern Roman Empire on a large scale
v.s: The Hun kings Attila and Bleda jointly led their army across the Danube, invading the Balkan provinces of the Eastern Roman Empire in force. The Hun cavalry swept through Thrace, Macedonia, and other regions, sacking over seventy cities, and advanced to the very walls of Constantinople. Eastern Roman Emperor Theodosius II was forced to sue for peace, agreeing to pay a huge sum of gold, cede territories south of the Danube, and pay an annual tribute.

v.t: Northern Liang king Juqu Mengxun attacks Western Qin, captures Fuhan
v.s: Taking advantage of internal disorder in Western Qin after its destruction by Xia, Juqu Mengxun, king of Northern Liang, led an army to attack the remaining Western Qin forces, capturing the important military stronghold of Fuhan (modern Linxia, Gansu). Northern Liang now controlled the upper Yellow River and parts of Longxi, greatly increasing its power and becoming the strongest regime in the Hexi Corridor.

d.u: Emperor Wen of Song, Liu Yilong

v.t: Saint Patrick begins missionary work in Ireland
v.s: The Christian missionary Patrick (later known as Saint Patrick) traveled from Gaul to Ireland and began large-scale missionary activity. Patrick successfully converted many Irish tribal chiefs and kings to Christianity, and established churches, monasteries, and bishoprics across the island. Ireland's unique environment, largely untouched by Roman influence, led to the development of a distinctive form of Irish monasticism.

v.t: Zhao Guang rebellion breaks out in Liu Song's Yizhou, lasting six months
v.s: Due to the harsh governance of the inspector of Yizhou (modern Sichuan and Chongqing area), the people suffered heavy burdens, leading to a peasant rebellion led by Zhao Guang. The rebels briefly captured several counties around Chengdu and gathered tens of thousands of followers. The Liu Song court sent troops to suppress the rebellion, which was crushed after half a year of fighting. This rebellion exposed the shortcomings of Liu Song local governance.

v.t: Hun kings Attila and Bleda again invade the Eastern Roman Empire
v.s: Claiming that the Eastern Roman Empire had not fully paid the gold tribute according to the treaty, the Hun kings Attila and Bleda again led their army across the Danube. The Huns sacked cities in Illyria and Thrace, forcing Emperor Theodosius II to double the annual tribute. The Eastern Roman treasury was plunged into crisis.

d.u: Emperor Wen of Song, Liu Yilong

v.t: Eastern Roman Empire pays huge annual tribute to the Huns in exchange for peace
v.s: Unable to resist the Huns, Eastern Roman Emperor Theodosius II reached a new treaty with Attila and Bleda. The Eastern Roman Empire would pay 700 pounds of gold annually to the Huns, as well as ransom for many captives. This was the first time the Eastern Roman Empire formally paid an annual tribute to a barbarian chieftain. Attila used this wealth to dominate central Europe, establishing a vast nomadic empire.

v.t: Liu Song poet Xie Lingyun executed by Emperor Wen of Song
v.s: Xie Lingyun, a landscape poet of the Liu Song dynasty and grandson of Xie Xuan, became involved in the power struggles of the Liu Song imperial clan. Emperor Wen of Song ordered his execution on charges of treason. Before his death, Xie Lingyun wrote verses lamenting that 'a brilliant writer may not keep his life.' He was 49. Xie Lingyun founded the landscape poetry school in Chinese literary history, deeply influencing later poets such as Wang Wei and Meng Haoran.

v.t: Visigothic Kingdom under Theodoric I continues expansion
v.s: Taking advantage of the weakness of the Western Roman Empire, Theodoric I led his army north, capturing cities in southeastern Gaul such as Narbonne and Arles. The Visigothic kingdom expanded from Aquitaine in Gaul to the Mediterranean coast, becoming one of the most powerful barbarian kingdoms in Western Europe.

d.u: Emperor Wen of Song, Liu Yilong

v.t: Continued conflict between Liu Song and Northern Wei in the Ru and Ying river valleys
v.s: Border friction between Liu Song and Northern Wei continued to escalate, with repeated skirmishes in the Ru River (modern Ruhe, Henan) and Ying River (modern Yinghe, Anhui) basins. Liu Song generals Dao Yanzhi and Wang Zhongde resisted, but mostly defended, failing to break through the Northern Wei lines. The pattern of confrontation between the Northern and Southern Dynasties largely took shape.

v.t: Hun king Bleda dies; Attila takes sole power
v.s: The joint Hun king Bleda died suddenly during a hunt (rumors suggested Attila had him assassinated). Attila became the sole ruler of the Hun Empire and immediately centralized power, conquering regions such as Styria and Cappadocia. The Hun Empire entered the Age of Attila. Attila was known for his cruelty and cunning, called the 'Scourge of God' by Europeans.

v.t: Eastern Roman Empire builds Danube frontier defenses to counter Huns
v.s: Eastern Roman Emperor Theodosius II ordered the construction of hundreds of kilometers of forts and walls along the Danube in the north to defend against Hun cavalry incursions. This line of defense, known as the 'Danube Limes,' consisted of dozens of fortresses and ramparts garrisoned by tens of thousands of troops. However, the highly mobile Hun cavalry often bypassed the defenses, crossing at multiple fords.

d.u: Emperor Wen of Song, Liu Yilong

v.t: Eastern Roman Empire issues Theodosian Code
v.s: The Theodosian Code, ordered compiled by Eastern Roman Emperor Theodosius II, was formally issued. This code collected all the edicts issued by Roman emperors from Constantine I to Theodosius II, covering a hundred years, in sixteen books covering administration, criminal law, civil law, religion, and more. It became the foundation of Eastern Roman law and significantly influenced the development of medieval European law.

v.t: Emperor Taiwu of Northern Wei leads campaign against Northern Yan, besieges Helong
v.s: Emperor Taiwu of Northern Wei led an army east to attack the Northern Yan regime that had seceded in western Liaoning. The Wei army captured Yingzhou and other areas, advancing to besiege Helong (modern Chaoyang, Liaoning), the Northern Yan capital. Northern Yan king Feng Hong was forced to sue for peace and submit as a vassal to Northern Wei. The destruction of Northern Yan was now only a matter of time.

v.t: Vandal king Gaiseric sends fleet to plunder Sicily and southern Italy
v.s: Vandal king Gaiseric sent the Vandal navy from Carthage to raid the coasts of Sicily and southern Italy. The Vandal fleet sacked Palermo, Syracuse, and other cities, even threatening Rome. The Western Roman Empire was unable to counter and was forced to cede western Sicily to the Vandal Kingdom.

d.u: Emperor Wen of Song, Liu Yilong

v.t: Northern Wei captures Helong, destroys Northern Yan
v.s: Emperor Taiwu of Northern Wei again led his army to besiege Helong (modern Chaoyang, Liaoning), capital of Northern Yan. Northern Yan king Feng Hong abandoned the city and fled to Goguryeo, and Northern Yan was destroyed. Northern Wei annexed western and eastern Liaoning, taking full control of northeastern China and extending its influence to the Korean border.

v.t: Burgundians establish Burgundian Kingdom in southeastern Gaul
v.s: The Burgundian king Gundioc led his people, taking advantage of the decline of the Western Roman Empire, to establish the Burgundian Kingdom in the Rhône and Saône river valleys of southeastern Gaul, with its capital at Lyon. The Burgundians adhered to Arian Christianity, creating religious differences with the Gallo-Roman population. The Burgundian Kingdom was later absorbed by the Frankish Kingdom.

v.t: Hun king Attila invades Eastern Roman Empire, advances to Constantinople
v.s: Attila led a large army in a major invasion of the Eastern Roman Empire, capturing cities such as Serdica (modern Sofia, Bulgaria) and Philippopolis (modern Plovdiv, Bulgaria), with his vanguard reaching Hadrianople outside Constantinople. Emperor Theodosius II urgently reinforced the walls and sent envoys to negotiate peace. Lacking siege engines, Attila accepted talks and withdrew.

d.u: Emperor Wen of Song, Liu Yilong

v.t: Emperor Wen of Song establishes Four Academies, greatly develops scholarship
v.s: Emperor Wen of Song established four academies in Jiankang: Confucian, Metaphysical, Historical, and Literary, taught by renowned scholars Lei Cizong, He Shangzhi, Pei Songzhi, and Xie Yuan respectively. The establishment of the four academies trained many talents, promoting the fusion of Wei-Jin metaphysics, history, literature, and Confucianism, making the Yuanjia period one of the most culturally prosperous periods of the Southern Dynasties.

v.t: Eastern Roman Emperor Theodosius II marries Athenian scholar Eudocia
v.s: Theodosius II married Eudocia, a philosopher and former pagan scholar from Athens. After converting to Christianity, Eudocia became empress. She promoted literature and the arts, fostering a cultural flourishing in Constantinople and helping to preserve and spread classical Greek culture in Byzantium.

v.t: Eastern Roman Empire repels a Hun attack; Attila is thwarted
v.s: Attila again led his army across the Danube to attack the Eastern Roman Empire. The Eastern Roman army set up defenses on the Utus River (modern Vit River) south of the Danube, using terrain and archers to resist the Hun cavalry. The Huns suffered heavy casualties and were forced to retreat. This was one of Attila's rare defeats, temporarily relieving pressure on the Eastern Roman Empire.

d.u: Emperor Wen of Song, Liu Yilong

v.t: Theodosian Code takes effect, becomes basis of Eastern Roman law
v.s: The Theodosian Code, ordered compiled by Theodosius II, formally came into force throughout the Eastern Roman Empire. The code systematically organized the development of Roman law since Constantine the Great, laying the foundation for the later Code of Justinian and holding an important place in European legal history.

v.t: Northern Liang king Juqu Mengxun dies; Northern Liang begins to decline
v.s: Juqu Mengxun, founding king of Northern Liang, died of illness and was succeeded by his son Juqu Mujian. Juqu Mengxun was an outstanding politician and military strategist of the Sixteen Kingdoms period, having unified the Hexi Corridor and for a time controlled the Western Regions. Juqu Mujian was far less capable than his father; Northern Liang wavered between Northern Wei and Rouran, and began to decline.

v.t: Hun king Attila invades Thessaly and central Greece
v.s: Attila led a large army around the Eastern Roman Danube defenses, crossed the Balkan Mountains, and invaded Thessaly (central Greece). The Hun cavalry sacked Larissa, Thebes, and other cities, reaching Thermopylae. The Eastern Roman Empire was powerless to resist, retreating to the Isthmus of Corinth. Attila returned north after plundering great treasure in Greece.

d.u: Emperor Wen of Song, Liu Yilong

v.t: Emperor Taiwu of Northern Wei leads western expedition, destroys Northern Liang, unifies north
v.s: Emperor Taiwu of Northern Wei, using the pretext of 'Northern Liang colluding with Rouran,' personally led a large army from Pingcheng on a thousand-li lightning strike against the Northern Liang capital Guzang (modern Wuwei, Gansu). Northern Liang king Juqu Mujian surrendered, and Northern Liang was destroyed. Northern Wei took control of the Hexi Corridor, opened the route to the Western Regions, ended the fragmentation of the Sixteen Kingdoms, and unified northern China.

v.t: Vandal king Gaiseric captures Carthage, establishes Vandal Kingdom
v.s: Vandal king Gaiseric led his army to capture Carthage, the capital of Roman North Africa, and formally established the Vandal Kingdom there. Gaiseric confiscated lands of Roman aristocrats and the Catholic Church, distributing them to Vandal warriors. The Vandals adhered to Arian Christianity and subjected Catholics to religious discrimination and persecution. The Roman Empire's most important granary fell into barbarian hands, leading to economic collapse.

v.t: Goguryeo King Jangsu moves capital to Pyongyang
v.s: Jangsu (Go Geon), the twentieth ruler of Goguryeo, moved the capital from Guknaeseong (modern Ji'an, Jilin) to Pyongyang (modern Pyongyang, North Korea). King Jangsu reigned for seventy-nine years, the longest in Goguryeo history. Moving the capital to Pyongyang brought Goguryeo closer to the southern part of the Korean Peninsula, facilitating control over Baekje and Silla, ushering in Goguryeo's most prosperous period.

d.u: Emperor Wen of Song, Liu Yilong

v.t: Vandal king Gaiseric occupies Sicily, controls western Mediterranean
v.s: Vandal king Gaiseric sent his fleet to occupy the entire island of Sicily, extending Vandal naval power from North Africa to southern Italy and Sicily. The Vandals controlled the shipping routes of the western Mediterranean, cutting Rome's maritime links with Africa and Spain. Western Roman maritime trade nearly ground to a halt.

v.t: Northern Liang remnants launch revolt in Hexi Corridor
v.s: After Northern Wei occupied the Hexi Corridor, some remaining Northern Liang nobles and Buddhist monks launched a pro-Liang revolt in Zhangye, Jiuquan, and elsewhere. The revolt gained support from Han Chinese magnates in the Hexi region and grew large. Emperor Taiwu of Northern Wei sent a large force to suppress it, killing thousands of participants after months of fighting.

v.t: Yazdegerd II of Persia ascends throne, intensifies persecution of Christians
v.s: Sassanian king Yazdegerd II, seeking to consolidate Zoroastrianism as the state religion, ordered a large-scale persecution of Christians in his empire. Many Nestorian Christians fled to the Eastern Roman Empire for refuge. Persia's religious persecution increased tensions with the Eastern Roman Empire.

d.u: Emperor Wen of Song, Liu Yilong

v.t: War between Eastern Roman Empire and Huns resumes; Balkans ravaged
v.s: Using the excuse that the Eastern Roman Empire had not paid the tribute on time, Attila again led a large army into the Balkans. The Huns captured and destroyed over seventy cities, including Belgrade, Niš, and Sofia, taking many captives. Emperor Theodosius II urgently recalled troops from Syria and Egypt, but they could not stop the Hun cavalry's sweep.

v.t: Northern Wei dispatches troops to destroy Qiuci state, controlling Longnan
v.s: Emperor Taiwu of Northern Wei sent General Xi Jin to attack the Qiuci state (modern Longnan, Gansu). Qiuci ruler Yang Baozong was defeated and surrendered, and the Qiuci state was destroyed. Northern Wei took control of the strategic mountain passes of Longnan and northern Hanzhong, semi-encircling Liu Song from the west.

v.t: Vandal king Gaiseric signs treaty with Visigothic Kingdom
v.s: To avoid a two-front war, Vandal king Gaiseric signed a treaty with the Visigothic Kingdom, agreeing not to attack each other. The treaty recognized Vandal control over North Africa, Sardinia, and Sicily, and Visigothic control over Spain and southern Gaul, with the two kingdoms dividing the Western Roman Empire's legacy in the western Mediterranean.

d.u: Emperor Wen of Song, Liu Yilong

v.t: Eastern Roman Empire signs treaty with Huns; annual tribute increased to 2,100 pounds of gold
v.s: Unable to continue resisting the Huns, Eastern Roman Emperor Theodosius II signed a treaty with Attila. The Eastern Roman Empire would pay the Huns an annual tribute increased from 700 to 2,100 pounds of gold, and cede a 300-li-wide buffer zone south of the Danube. Attila used this wealth to continue expanding, becoming the overlord of central and eastern Europe.

v.t: Liu Song Prince Liu Jun stationed at Jingkou, strengthens northern defenses
v.s: Emperor Wen of Song sent his son Liu Jun to garrison Jingkou (modern Zhenjiang, Jiangsu), responsible for the defense of the Huainan and Jiangbei regions. Liu Jun reorganized the military, building dozens of beacon towers and forts along the Huai River, strengthening Liu Song's northern border defenses.

v.t: Saxons begin large-scale invasions of eastern Britain
v.s: The Germanic tribes of Saxons, Angles, and Jutes began crossing from the European continent in large numbers to invade the eastern coasts of Britain. They advanced inland along the Thames, Humber, and other rivers, establishing early kingdoms such as Essex, Sussex, and Wessex. The Roman-British population was forced to retreat west into Wales and Cornwall. This process lasted about 150 years and is known as the 'Anglo-Saxon conquest.'

d.u: Emperor Wen of Song, Liu Yilong

v.t: Hun king Attila conquers the interior of the Balkan Peninsula
v.s: Attila led his army to conquer the tribes of the interior of the Balkan Peninsula, including Gepids, Skiri, Heruli, and other Germanic tribes as well as Sarmatian nomads. He incorporated these tribes into the Hun Empire, requiring them to provide troops and supplies. The Hun Empire's sphere of influence stretched from Central Europe to the Tigris River (modern Iraq).

v.t: Emperor Taiwu of Northern Wei again campaigns north against Rouran; Rouran khagan Tughuzhen flees north
v.s: Emperor Taiwu of Northern Wei personally led one hundred thousand cavalry on his sixth northern campaign against Rouran. The Wei forces penetrated 5,000 li into the desert, heavily defeating the Rouran army at the Jinshan (modern Altai Mountains). Rouran khagan Tughuzhen fled north with only a few thousand horsemen, and hundreds of thousands surrendered. Rouran was crippled and no longer posed a serious threat to Northern Wei.

v.t: Baekje's King Biyu maintains close contacts with the Southern Dynasties
v.s: King Biyu of Baekje maintained close diplomatic relations with the Liu Song of the Southern Dynasties, sending envoys frequently to present tribute. Baekje traded with Liu Song by sea, studying cultural systems and Buddhist scriptures from the Southern Dynasties. Baekje served as a cultural bridge between China and the Japanese archipelago.

d.u: Emperor Wen of Song, Liu Yilong

v.t: Eastern Roman Empire sends envoys to Persia, trying to ally against Huns
v.s: Emperor Theodosius II sent envoys to the Sassanian Persian Empire, proposing an alliance to jointly counter the threat of the Huns and the Hephthalites. Persian king Yazdegerd II outwardly agreed but did not take action. The Eastern Roman strategy of trying to ally with Persia achieved little.

v.t: Hun king Attila gathers massive army, prepares for western campaign against Gaul
v.s: Attila gathered a massive army of 500,000, including Hun cavalry, Ostrogoths, Gepids, and other tribes, to launch a large-scale invasion of the Western Roman province of Gaul. His pretext was that Honoria, sister of Western Roman Emperor Valentinian III, had sent him a proposal of marriage. Attila's western campaign was about to shake the entire political order of Western Europe.

v.t: Liu Song court discusses another northern expedition but abandons it due to internal opposition
v.s: Seeing that the Northern Wei army was occupied in the west with the Northern Liang campaign and had left the south vulnerable, Emperor Wen of Song considered launching a northern expedition to recover the central plains. But ministers Wang Xuanmo and Jiang Zhan argued that Liu Song's national strength had not yet recovered, and a hasty expedition would fail. After debate, Emperor Wen abandoned the plan. This decision caused Liu Song to miss its last chance to recover the central plains.

d.u: Emperor Wen of Song, Liu Yilong

v.t: Hun king Attila fully unifies the Hun Empire; brother Bleda supposedly killed
v.s: The power struggle between Attila and Bleda ended in Attila's victory. Bleda was supposedly assassinated on Attila's orders. Attila became the sole ruler of the Hun Empire and immediately carried out military and political reforms, unifying all Hun, Germanic, and Sarmatian tribes under a centralized rule, preparing for his western campaign.

v.t: Lu Shui Hu chief Gai Wu revolts at Xingcheng, contacts Liu Song
v.s: Gai Wu, a chieftain of the Lu Shui Hu, launched a revolt at Xingcheng (modern Huangling, Shaanxi) within Northern Wei territory, proclaiming himself Heavenly King. The rebel force grew large, gathering over one hundred thousand people, capturing several counties in Guanzhong. Gai Wu sent envoys to Liu Song for aid, and Emperor Wen of Song appointed Gai Wu as Inspector of Yongzhou, but delayed sending troops. Emperor Taiwu of Northern Wei led his army to suppress the revolt, which ultimately failed.

v.t: Pope Leo I meets Attila, persuades him to abandon attack on Rome
v.s: After Western Roman Emperor Valentinian III fled Rome in panic, Pope Leo I went out to meet Attila. Using his personal charisma and religious authority, Leo successfully persuaded Attila to give up his planned attack on Rome. After receiving a large amount of gold, Attila withdrew, and Rome was temporarily spared. This event was later given a religious gloss by Christian historians, becoming an early basis for papal supremacy over imperial authority.

d.u: Emperor Wen of Song, Liu Yilong

v.t: Emperor Taiwu of Northern Wei issues edict to destroy Buddhism, burn scriptures and images
v.s: Under the influence of the Taoist Kou Qianzhi, whom he favored, and after discovering weapons and concealed women in Chang'an monasteries during the suppression of Gai Wu's rebellion, Emperor Taiwu of Northern Wei issued an edict to destroy Buddhism throughout the empire. The edict ordered the burning of Buddhist scriptures and images, the demolition of temples, and the execution of monks. This was the first large-scale persecution of Buddhism in Chinese history (the first of the 'Three Wu and One Zong' persecutions). Buddhism in the north was devastated, and many monks fled south to Liu Song.

v.t: Vandal king Gaiseric sacks Rome, carries away vast treasure
v.s: At the invitation of the Western Roman empress Eudoxia (who had been insulted by the new emperor Valentinian III after her husband's death), Vandal king Gaiseric led his fleet from North Africa to capture Rome. The Vandal army looted the city for fourteen days, taking the golden menorah from the Temple in Jerusalem (booty brought by Titus from Jerusalem in 70 AD), the gold of the Roman Senate, and countless other treasures, also capturing the empress and two princesses. This sack was even more thorough than the Visigothic sack of 410, and 'vandalism' became synonymous with the destruction of civilization.

v.t: Liu Song and Northern Wei clash in the Runan and Xincai region
v.s: Liu Song generals Dao Yanzhi and Wang Xuanmo clashed with Northern Wei forces in the Runan (modern Runan, Henan) and Xincai (modern Xincai, Henan) areas. Both sides had some victories and defeats, but Liu Song failed to break through the Northern Wei lines, and the Wei army did not cross the Huai River. This border conflict was a continuation of the Yuanjia-era north-south war.

d.u: Emperor Wen of Song, Liu Yilong

v.t: Hun king Attila again invades Eastern Roman Empire, advances to Constantinople
v.s: Using late payment of tribute as a pretext, Attila again led his army to invade the Eastern Roman Empire. The Hun cavalry broke through the Danube defenses and sacked Illyria, Thrace, and Macedonia, with the vanguard reaching the walls of Constantinople. Theodosius II was forced to sue for peace, increasing the annual tribute to 2,100 pounds of gold and ceding a 300-li-wide zone south of the Danube.

v.t: Emperor Yuryaku of Japan ascends; strengthens centralization
v.s: Emperor Yuryaku (also known as Ohatsuse Wakatakeru no Mikoto) of the Yamato court ascended the throne. He was a powerful ruler who conquered local magnates in the east and west, strengthening centralization. He sent envoys to the Liu Song court, which appointed him 'General Who Pacifies the East, King of Wa' (Japan).

v.t: Liu Song attacks Linyi (Champa), achieves great victory
v.s: Tan Hezhi, Inspector of Jiaozhou under Liu Song, was ordered to attack Linyi (Champa, modern central Vietnam), which had repeatedly raided the border. The Liu Song navy broke the capital of Linyi, and King Fan Yang Mai II sued for peace, offering gold, silver, treasures, and trained elephants. This victory established Liu Song's suzerainty over the coast of Southeast Asia.

d.u: Emperor Wen of Song, Liu Yilong

v.t: Eastern Roman Emperor Theodosius II dies; Marcian ascends
v.s: Theodosius II died of injuries from a riding accident at age 50. The Senate elected the comes domesticorum Marcian as emperor. Marcian reversed Theodosius II's policy of paying tribute to the Huns, announcing the cessation of payments and strengthening the defenses of Constantinople and the Danube. Attila was enraged upon hearing the news and prepared a western campaign.

v.t: Eastern Roman envoy sent to Huns; negotiations fail
