v.s: The new Eastern Roman emperor Marcian sent an envoy to the Hun court to attempt negotiations with Attila, but declared that he would not continue paying tribute. Attila was furious and threatened to attack Constantinople. However, he decided to first campaign west into Gaul, temporarily leaving the Eastern Roman Empire alone.

v.t: Emperor Taiwu of Northern Wei launches sixth campaign against Rouran, conquers northern Tianshan
v.s: Emperor Taiwu of Northern Wei led his army north against Rouran, penetrating deep into the desert, crossing the Tianshan Mountains, and reaching the Western Regions. The Rouran khagan Tughuzhen fled far away, and the Wei forces captured countless Rouran tribesmen and livestock, essentially eliminating the Rouran threat to Northern Wei's northern border.

d.u: Emperor Wen of Song, Liu Yilong

v.t: Liu Song launches massive northern expedition — the second Yuanjia northern expedition
v.s: Ignoring opposition from his ministers, Emperor Wen of Song dispatched a force of 300,000 under the overall command of Wang Xuanmo, divided into three routes, for a massive northern expedition against Northern Wei. The Liu Song army temporarily recaptured Huatai, Luoyang, and Hulao. But Wang Xuanmo made strategic errors while besieging Huatai, and Emperor Taiwu of Northern Wei led his army south in a counterattack. The Liu Song forces collapsed completely, suffering heavy losses, exhausting the economic strength accumulated during the Yuanjia administration.

v.t: Hun king Attila gathers massive army, prepares for western campaign against Gaul (449)
v.s: Attila gathered a massive army of 500,000, including Hun cavalry, Ostrogoths, Gepids, Skiri, and other tribes, declaring a western campaign into Gaul (modern France) to attack the Visigothic Kingdom and the Western Roman Empire. The outcome of this war would determine the early medieval political map of Europe.

v.t: Emperor Yuryaku of Japan ascends, strengthens centralization
v.s: The strong ruler of the Yamato court, Emperor Yuryaku (also known as Great King Wu), acceded to the throne. During his reign, he conquered various local chieftains in the east and west, strengthening centralization. He sent envoys to Liu Song, which conferred upon him the title 'General Who Pacifies the East, King of Wa.'

d.u: Emperor Wen of Song, Liu Yilong

v.t: Liu Song's Yuanjia northern expedition ends in disaster; Jiangbei region savagely ravaged
v.s: After the crushing defeat of the Liu Song expeditionary force at Huatai and Luoyang, Emperor Taiwu of Northern Wei personally led a large army south, attacking the Liu Song's Jiangbei region. The Wei forces captured Xuanhu, Xiangcheng, and other towns, reaching Guabu (modern Luhe, Nanjing), threatening Jiankang across the river. The Wei army committed large-scale massacres in Jiangbei, causing heavy casualties, and Liu Song's vitality was greatly damaged. The 'Yuanjia Administration' thus came to an end.

v.t: Hun king Attila dies suddenly; empire quickly disintegrates
v.s: Attila died from a massive nosebleed on his wedding night after marrying the Germanic princess Ildico. After his death, his sons fought for the throne, and the conquered Germanic tribes rose in revolt. The Hun Empire fell apart within just a few years and disappeared from history.

v.t: Irish monks discover Iceland
v.s: In his book 'On the Measurement of the Earth', the Irish monk Dicuil recorded that some Irish monks sailed north and discovered an island they called 'Thule' (believed to be Iceland). These monks stayed on the island for several months, noting that there was no summer night, but the island was covered with snow and ice in winter. This was the earliest known European discovery of Iceland.

d.u: Emperor Wen of Song, Liu Yilong

v.t: Battle of the Catalaunian Plains: Roman-Visigothic coalition defeats Attila
v.s: Attila led 500,000 Huns into Gaul and besieged Orléans. The Western Roman general Flavius Aetius, in alliance with Visigothic king Theodoric I, led a Roman-Visigothic relief army. The two sides met on the Catalaunian Plains (modern Champagne, France) in a decisive battle involving over 100,000 men. During the fierce fighting, Theodoric I was killed, but the coalition eventually repelled the Hun cavalry. Attila retreated into his camp, stacking saddles to commit suicide, but the coalition halted pursuit and let him go. The Battle of the Catalaunian Plains was one of the decisive battles of European history, preventing the Huns from conquering Western Europe.

v.t: Council of Chalcedon: Dyophysitism becomes orthodox
v.s: Eastern Roman Emperor Marcian convened the fourth ecumenical council at Chalcedon (modern Kadıköy, Turkey). The council declared that Christ has two natures, divine and human, 'without confusion, without change, without division, without separation,' thus reconciling the Alexandrian and Antiochene schools. The Chalcedonian Creed became orthodox Christian doctrine.

v.t: Emperor Taiwu of Northern Wei leads southern campaign, reaches Guabu, threatens Jiankang
v.s: Emperor Taiwu of Northern Wei led his army south, crossing the Huai River and reaching Guabu (modern Luhe, Nanjing), facing the Liu Song capital Jiankang across the Yangtze. Emperor Wen of Song ordered Jiankang to be placed on alert, causing panic among the populace. Lacking boats and facing hot weather, the Northern Wei army withdrew north. On the retreat, Taiwu pillaged the six Jiangbei provinces, inflicting devastating damage on Liu Song's economy.

d.u: Emperor Wen of Song, Liu Yilong

v.t: Emperor Taiwu of Northern Wei murdered by eunuch Zong Ai; Emperor Wencheng ascends
v.s: Emperor Taiwu of Northern Wei's favor for the eunuch Zong Ai allowed the latter to become extremely powerful. When Taiwu discovered that Zong Ai had framed the crown prince Tuoba Huang and sought to execute Zong Ai, Zong Ai struck first, having Taiwu assassinated at age 45. Zong Ai then installed Taiwu's youngest son Tuoba Han as emperor, but then killed him as well, finally establishing Taiwu's grandson Tuoba Jun as emperor, known as Emperor Wencheng. After ascending, Wencheng executed Zong Ai and began to restore Buddhism.

v.t: Attila's sons fight for succession; empire fragments
v.s: Attila's sons Ellac, Dengizich, and Ernak fought for the throne. The conquered Germanic tribes, including the Gepids, Skiri, and Ostrogoths, rose in rebellion. Ellac was killed in battle. Dengizich led his remnants to the lower Danube. The Hun Empire quickly disintegrated and vanished from the historical stage.

v.t: Liu Song crown prince Liu Shao conflicts with Emperor Wen; political turmoil
v.s: Emperor Wen of Song had a serious conflict with his crown prince Liu Shao over witchcraft. Liu Shao colluded with the sorceress Yan Daoyu to curse the emperor to an early death. When the plot was discovered, the emperor planned to depose the prince. Liu Shao launched a preemptive coup, killing Emperor Wen. Liu Shao proclaimed himself emperor, but faced opposition from imperial clansmen and officials, plunging Liu Song into civil war.

d.u: Emperor Wen of Song, Liu Yilong

v.t: Liu Song civil war: Liu Jun raises troops to attack Liu Shao
v.s: After Emperor Wen of Song was killed by Crown Prince Liu Shao, Emperor Wen's third son Liu Jun raised troops in Jiangzhou (modern Jiujiang, Jiangxi) to attack Liu Shao. Liu Jun gained the support of imperial clansmen and regional inspectors including Liu Yixuan of Jingzhou and Zang Zhi of Yongzhou, and marched on Jiankang. Liu Shao defended the capital, and the two sides fought for months.

v.t: Ostrogoths rise in Pannonia, throw off Hun control
v.s: After Attila's death, the Ostrogoths, under their chieftain Valamir, revolted against the Huns and defeated Attila's son Dengizich. The Ostrogoths broke free of Hun control, occupied Pannonia (modern Hungary), and established an independent Ostrogothic kingdom, later conquering Italy under Theodoric the Great.

v.t: Vandal king Gaiseric dies; Huneric succeeds
v.s: Gaiseric, the founder of the Vandal Kingdom and an outstanding military leader, died in Carthage at about age 81. His son Huneric succeeded him. Gaiseric had reigned for nearly fifty years, building a powerful Mediterranean maritime empire that destroyed Rome's naval hegemony. Huneric, of mediocre ability, continued persecution of Catholics during his reign.

d.u: Emperor Wen of Song, Liu Yilong

v.t: Liu Jun captures Jiankang, executes Liu Shao, ascends throne
v.s: Liu Jun's rebel army broke into Jiankang; Crown Prince Liu Shao was captured and executed. Liu Jun proclaimed himself emperor in Jiankang, known as Emperor Xiaowu of Song. To consolidate his throne, Xiaowu carried out large-scale purges of imperial clansmen and meritorious officials, and infighting among the Liu Song imperial house grew increasingly intense.

v.t: Western Roman general Aetius murdered by Emperor Valentinian III
v.s: Emperor Valentinian III, jealous of the prestige and power of his general Aetius, murdered him in the palace with his own hands. Aetius had led the coalition that defeated Attila at the Catalaunian Plains and was the last defender of the Western Roman Empire. After his death, the Western Roman Empire had no outstanding generals left and rapidly collapsed.

v.t: Hun Dengizich invades Eastern Roman Empire; defeated and killed
v.s: Attila's son Dengizich led his remaining forces to invade the Eastern Roman Empire, attempting to seize the lower Danube region. Eastern Roman Emperor Marcian sent an army that defeated the Huns, and Dengizich was killed. The Eastern Romans displayed his head in the Hippodrome of Constantinople. Attila's family line fell into obscurity.

d.u: Emperor Xiaowu of Song, Liu Jun

v.t: Vandal army sacks Rome, carries off imperial treasure
v.s: At the invitation of Empress Eudoxia, Vandal king Gaiseric led his army to sack Rome, looting the city for fourteen days. The Vandals seized the gold of the Roman Senate, the treasures of the Temple of Jupiter, and the golden menorah from the Temple in Jerusalem (booty brought by Titus in 70 AD). Rome suffered even greater destruction than in 410.

v.t: Emperor Xiaowu of Song suppresses rebellion of Prince of Jingling, Liu Dan
v.s: The Prince of Jingling, Liu Dan (brother of Emperor Xiaowu of Song), based at Guangling (modern Yangzhou, Jiangsu), plotted rebellion. Xiaowu sent a large force to besiege Guangling. After the city fell, Liu Dan was executed. Xiaowu took the opportunity to massacre many imperial clansmen and meritorious officials, further weakening the Liu Song imperial house.

v.t: Western Roman Emperor Valentinian III killed; empire in name only
v.s: Valentinian III was killed in Rome by followers of the late Aetius, at age 35. Eastern Roman Emperor Marcian refused to recognize Valentinian's son as successor and instead installed Petronius Maximus as emperor. Maximus reigned only 77 days before being killed by the populace when the Vandals sacked Rome. Thereafter, the Western Roman throne frequently changed hands; real power fell into the hands of barbarian generals, and the empire existed only in name.

d.u: Emperor Xiaowu of Song, Liu Jun

v.t: Visigothic king Theodoric II conquers most of Spain
v.s: Theodoric II led his army across the Pyrenees in a major attack on the Suebic Kingdom and remaining Vandal forces in Spain. The Visigoths captured Toledo, Mérida, and other important cities, taking control of most of the Iberian Peninsula. The Visigothic Kingdom became the most powerful barbarian kingdom in Western Europe.

v.t: Intra-imperial clan strife in Liu Song; several princes killed
v.s: Emperor Xiaowu of Song, suspicious of his imperial clansmen, successively killed the princes of Jingling, Liu Dan, and of Wuchang, Liu Hun, among others. The Liu Song imperial clan was devastated, and court power gradually fell into the hands of military men of humble origin. The foundation of Liu Song rule weakened and began to decline.

v.t: Eastern Roman Emperor Marcian dies; Leo I ascends
v.s: Marcian died, and Leo I, a Thracian soldier, was elected emperor by the Senate. Leo was the first Eastern Roman emperor to be proclaimed by the army. He set about military reforms, trying to restore imperial influence in the West.

d.u: Emperor Xiaowu of Song, Liu Jun

v.t: Eastern Roman Emperor Leo I crowned, establishes Leonid dynasty
v.s: Leo I was formally crowned in Constantinople, establishing the Leonid dynasty. Leo was a commoner who rose through the army. He tried to reduce the power of barbarian generals and promote native officials. His son-in-law would be the later emperor Zeno.

v.t: Emperor Xiaowu of Song implements new policies to weaken aristocratic clans
v.s: To weaken the political power of the aristocratic clans, Emperor Xiaowu greatly promoted men of humble origins and lower-level gentry to important posts, and reformed the official system, establishing low-level positions such as Zhongshu tongshe ren. These measures touched the interests of the aristocratic clans, intensifying internal contradictions, but also tilted Liu Song's ruling base toward the humble class.

v.t: Suebic king Rechiarius defeated and executed by Visigoths
v.s: Theodoric II defeated the Suebic king Rechiarius in northwestern Spain, capturing and executing him. The Suebic kingdom was forced to submit to the Visigoths, and most of the Iberian Peninsula fell under Visigothic rule.

d.u: Emperor Xiaowu of Song, Liu Jun

v.t: Emperor Wencheng of Northern Wei restores Buddhism; excavation of Yungang Grottoes begins
v.s: Emperor Wencheng of Northern Wei issued an edict to restore Buddhism, ending the persecution of Buddhism that began under Emperor Taiwu. He ordered the monk Tan Yao to carve the Yungang Grottoes at Mount Wuzhou west of Pingcheng (modern Datong, Shanxi). Tan Yao oversaw the excavation of the 'Five Caves of Tan Yao' (Caves 16–20), carving colossal Buddha figures symbolizing the five Northern Wei emperors. The excavation of the Yungang Grottoes marked the beginning of the revival of Buddhism in the north.

v.t: Eastern Roman Emperor Leo I rebuilds navy to counter Vandals
v.s: To retake North Africa, Emperor Leo I ordered a massive shipbuilding program to rebuild the Eastern Roman navy. He sent fleets to patrol the Mediterranean, attempting to cut the Vandal Kingdom's links with Europe. This was the first attempt by the Eastern Roman Empire to counter Vandal maritime supremacy with naval power.

v.t: Liu Song general Shen Qingzhi suppresses peasant rebellion in eastern Zhejiang
v.s: In the eastern Zhejiang region of Liu Song, heavy taxation led to a peasant rebellion. The rebel leader proclaimed himself emperor and gathered tens of thousands of followers. Emperor Xiaowu sent the veteran general Shen Qingzhi to suppress the rebellion. After months of fighting, the rebellion failed. Shen Qingzhi had tens of thousands of rebels killed, increasing popular resentment in eastern Zhejiang.

d.u: Emperor Xiaowu of Song, Liu Jun

v.t: Eastern Roman expedition against Vandals fails catastrophically; navy destroyed
v.s: Emperor Leo I launched a massive expedition against the Vandal Kingdom, deploying over 1,000 ships and 100,000 troops. Off the North African coast, Vandal king Gaiseric used fireships and incendiary tactics to destroy most of the Eastern Roman fleet. The expedition was almost completely wiped out, costing nearly half the Eastern Roman treasury. It was one of the worst military disasters in Eastern Roman history.

v.t: Shen Qingzhi again suppresses rebellion of Prince of Jingling, Liu Dan
v.s: The Prince of Jingling, Liu Dan (brother of Emperor Xiaowu), formally rebelled at Guangling (modern Yangzhou, Jiangsu). Xiaowu sent Shen Qingzhi with a large force to besiege Guangling. Shen used tunneling, fire attacks, and other tactics, and finally captured the city after months. Liu Dan was executed, and Xiaowu ordered a massacre, killing all adult males in the city. Liu Song intra-clan fighting became even more brutal.

v.t: Frankish king Childeric I dies; Clovis I ascends
v.s: Childeric I, king of the Salian Franks, died and was succeeded by his fifteen-year-old son Clovis I. Clovis was the most outstanding monarch of the Merovingian dynasty. He later unified the Frankish tribes, established the Frankish Kingdom, and converted to Christianity, founding the first dynasty of French history.

d.u: Emperor Xiaowu of Song, Liu Jun

v.t: Large-scale excavation of Yungang Grottoes makes it a center of Buddhist art in northern China
v.s: Following the order of Emperor Wencheng of Northern Wei, the monk Tan Yao continued the large-scale excavation of the Yungang Grottoes at Mount Wuzhou, Pingcheng. The Yungang sculptures blend Gandharan art with Xianbei culture, featuring massive, vigorous figures. Middle-period caves (Caves 5–13) introduced double-chamber caves and central pillars, with motifs such as 'two Buddhas seated together' and Vimalakīrti, reflecting the prosperity of Northern Wei Buddhism. Yungang is the earliest large-scale grotto complex in northern China and profoundly influenced Longmen and Dunhuang.

v.t: Hephthalites (White Huns) attack Sassanian Persia; Persia submits and pays tribute
v.s: The Central Asian nomadic Hephthalites (White Huns) launched a major invasion of the Sassanian Persian Empire. Persian king Peroz I was defeated and forced to submit to the Hephthalites, pay tribute, and send a prince as hostage. The Hephthalites controlled Central Asia and northeastern Persia, establishing a powerful nomadic empire.

v.t: Pipa enters central China via the Silk Road
v.s: The pear-shaped plucked lute known as the pipa, originating in Persia and Central Asia, was introduced into central China via the Silk Road through the Western Regions. The pipa's resonating body is pear-shaped, with frets on the neck and fingerboard, four strings, and is played with a plectrum or fingers. It quickly gained popularity in central Chinese courts and among commoners. The pipa appears extensively in Northern Dynasties cave paintings. The Tang dynasty poet Bai Juyi's 'Song of the Pipa' made it one of China's most expressive national instruments. The pipa's introduction is a classic example of cultural exchange along the Silk Road.

d.u: Emperor Xiaowu of Song, Liu Jun

v.t: Mutual slaughter among Liu Song imperial princes
v.s: After Emperor Xiaowu of Song died, his crown prince Liu Ziye ascended the throne, known as Emperor Qianfei of Song. Qianfei was dissolute, cruel, and suspicious of imperial clansmen, killing his uncles Liu Yigong, Liu Yu, and others. Liu Song intra-clan strife escalated; the court fell into chaos, and regional inspectors began to rebel against Qianfei.

v.t: Visigothic king Theodoric II conquers the Suebic Kingdom
v.s: Theodoric II led his army to completely conquer the Suebic Kingdom, expelling its king Rechiar from the Iberian Peninsula. The Visigothic Kingdom now entirely controlled Spain. Theodoric II then attacked the Franks in northern Gaul, expanding Visigothic influence.

v.t: Goguryeo King Jangsu establishes diplomatic relations with Northern Wei
v.s: King Jangsu of Goguryeo sent envoys to present tribute to Northern Wei. Emperor Wencheng of Northern Wei conferred upon him the title 'King of Goguryeo.' The establishment of formal diplomatic relations between Goguryeo and Northern Wei marked Goguryeo's recognition of Northern Wei's suzerainty in the north while maintaining independence.

d.u: Emperor Xiaowu of Song, Liu Jun

v.t: Zu Chongzhi of Liu Song compiles the Daming Calendar, accurately calculates tropical year
v.s: The Liu Song scientist Zu Chongzhi compiled the Daming Calendar. He determined the tropical year length as 365.24281481 days, only 46 seconds off modern measurements, and the draconic month as 27.21223 days, very close to today's value. The Daming Calendar introduced precession for the first time and improved intercalation, changing from 19 years with 7 intercalary months to 391 years with 144 intercalary months. It was the most advanced calendar of its time. However, due to opposition from Emperor Xiaowu's favorite courtier Dai Faxing, the Daming Calendar was not implemented until after Zu's death.

v.t: Eastern Roman Emperor Leo I implements monetary reform, restores gold coin credibility
v.s: Leo I reformed the currency, issuing solidus gold coins of high purity and strictly controlling counterfeiting. The reform solidus became an international hard currency in the Mediterranean world, circulating until the 11th century and laying the foundation for the Byzantine economy.

v.t: Frankish king Clovis I conquers Soissons, defeats last Roman forces
v.s: Clovis I defeated the Roman commander Syagrius in northern Gaul, occupying Soissons (modern Soissons, France). Clovis took control of the Paris basin and most of northern Gaul, moving the Frankish capital from Tournai to Soissons, laying the foundation for the later unification of Gaul.

d.u: Emperor Xiaowu of Song, Liu Jun

v.t: Emperor Xiaowu of Song dies; Emperor Qianfei of Song (Liu Ziye) ascends
v.s: Emperor Xiaowu of Song died of illness in Jiankang. His crown prince Liu Ziye ascended the throne, known as Emperor Qianfei of Song (or Former Deposed Emperor), at age sixteen. Liu Ziye was cruel and dissolute, engaging in egregious behavior, and suspicious of imperial clansmen and ministers, triggering total turmoil within Liu Song. His tyranny accelerated the dynasty's downfall.

v.t: Border clash between Eastern Roman Empire and Sassanian Persia in Asia Minor
v.s: The Eastern Roman Empire and Sassanian Persia clashed over Armenia in eastern Asia Minor. Both sides had victories and defeats, eventually maintaining the existing border through negotiation. The conflict was limited in scale but reflected the two empires' long-term competition in the Caucasus.

v.t: Vandal king Huneric dies; Gundobad succeeds
v.s: Vandal king Huneric died from a fall; his cousin Gundobad succeeded him. Gundobad continued the policy of persecuting Catholics, while strengthening control over Sardinia and Corsica. The Vandal Kingdom began to decline.

d.u: Emperor Xiaowu of Song, Liu Jun
Emperor Qianfei of Song, Liu Ziye

v.t: Emperor Qianfei of Song kills many imperial clansmen and ministers, alienating everyone
v.s: Emperor Qianfei of Song (Liu Ziye) indiscriminately killed imperial clansmen and court officials, including his grand-uncle Liu Yigong, his brother Liu Ziluan, and ministers Yan Shigu, Liu Jingxian, and others. He also imprisoned and humiliated his uncles Liu Yu and Liu Xiuren, causing all officials to fear for their lives. The Liu Song court fell into unprecedented terror.

v.t: Eastern Roman Emperor Leo I marries his daughter to Isaurian Zeno to ease internal politics
v.s: To gain the support of the military command in Illyria, Leo I married his daughter Ariadne to Zeno, an Isaurian. Zeno came from a chieftain family on the eastern frontier. This marriage strengthened Leo's ruling base but also set the stage for Zeno's later accession.

v.t: Frankish king Clovis I marries Burgundian princess Clotilde
v.s: Clovis I married Clotilde, a princess of the Burgundian Kingdom. Clotilde was a devout Catholic, and after the marriage she tried to persuade Clovis to abandon Germanic paganism and convert to Roman Catholicism. This marriage played a key role in the Christianization of the Frankish Kingdom.

d.u: Emperor Ming of Song, Liu Yu

v.t: Liu Yu kills Liu Ziye, proclaims himself emperor
v.s: The tyranny of Emperor Qianfei Liu Ziye provoked opposition from both within and without. The Prince of Xiangdong, Liu Yu, together with court officials Ruan Dianfu and Shou Jizhi, killed Liu Ziye at night. Liu Yu then ascended the throne, known as Emperor Ming of Song. However, the legitimacy of Liu Yu's accession was questioned by other imperial clansmen, triggering a full-scale civil war in Liu Song.

v.t: Ostrogoths enter Italy at the behest of the Western Roman emperor
v.s: The Ostrogothic king Theodoric the Great (then still a prince) led the Ostrogothic army into Italy, nominally at the request of Eastern Roman Emperor Zeno to help the Western Roman Empire deal with the barbarian mercenary leader Odoacer in Italy. Theodoric, in fact, intended to conquer Italy and establish an Ostrogothic kingdom.

v.t: Persian king Peroz I counterattacks the Hephthalites but is defeated and killed
v.s: Unwilling to submit to the Hephthalites, Peroz I led a large army to attack them. However, he fell into a Hephthalite ambush, and the Persian army was annihilated. Peroz was killed. Persia was forced to cede vast eastern territories and pay tribute to the Hephthalites.

d.u: Emperor Ming of Song, Liu Yu

v.t: Yijia Calamity in Liu Song: imperial princes unite against Emperor Ming
v.s: The accession of Emperor Ming of Song (Liu Yu), who had killed his predecessor, was widely opposed by imperial clansmen. The Prince of Jin'an, Liu Zixun, raised troops at Xunyang (modern Jiujiang, Jiangxi), supported by officials Deng Wan, Yuan Yi, and most regional inspectors. This was known as the 'Yijia Calamity.' Liu Yu based himself in Jiankang and sent troops to attack, plunging Liu Song into a large-scale civil war.

v.t: Northern Wei takes advantage of Liu Song's civil war to occupy Qingzhou and Xuzhou
v.s: Emperor Wencheng of Northern Wei, taking advantage of Liu Song's internal conflict, sent the general Murong Baiyao south to capture Liu Song's northern territories: Qingzhou (modern Shandong) and Xuzhou (modern Xuzhou, Jiangsu). Liu Song, preoccupied with civil war, was unable to resist, losing extensive northern territory, and the north-south front retreated to the Huai River line.

v.t: Eastern Roman Emperor Leo I dies; Zeno ascends
v.s: Leo I died of illness; his grandson Leo II succeeded, but the infant Leo II died only a few months later. Leo I's son-in-law, the Isaurian Zeno, became Eastern Roman emperor. Zeno, a barbarian by origin, was not recognized by the aristocracy of Constantinople, and the Eastern Roman Empire fell into civil war.

d.u: Emperor Ming of Song, Liu Yu

v.t: Emperor Ming of Song suppresses Yijia Calamity, massacres many imperial clansmen
v.s: Emperor Ming of Song sent the generals Xiao Daocheng and Zhang Yong to attack Liu Zixun. Liu Zixun was defeated and killed. Liu Yu then massively massacred imperial clansmen and officials who had participated in the rebellion, including Liu Zixun, Liu Zisui, Liu Zixu, and over ten other princes. The Liu Song imperial clan was nearly wiped out, clearing the way for Xiao Daocheng's usurpation.

v.t: Eastern Roman Emperor Zeno and General Basiliscus fight for the throne
v.s: Because Zeno's barbarian origin was opposed by the aristocracy and army in Constantinople, General Basiliscus rebelled, captured Constantinople, and proclaimed himself emperor. Zeno fled to Isauria, and the Eastern Roman Empire fell into civil war, allowing the Vandals and Ostrogoths to invade.

v.t: Last capable Western Roman emperor, Anthemius, ascends
v.s: Eastern Roman Emperor Leo I appointed Anthemius as Western Roman emperor, hoping he could restore order in the West. Anthemius, Leo I's son-in-law, had some military strength. He attempted to recover North Africa and Gaul, but it was too late; the Western Roman Empire's fall was inevitable.

d.u: Emperor Ming of Song, Liu Yu

v.t: Second Eastern Roman expedition against Vandals ends in disaster; national treasury exhausted
v.s: Emperor Leo I (who had not yet been restored after Basiliscus's coup) organized a second large-scale expedition against the Vandals, with over 1,000 ships and 100,000 troops. But Vandal king Gaiseric again used fire ships to trap the Eastern Roman fleet in the Bay of Tunis, destroying it almost completely. This failure exhausted the Eastern Roman treasury, and it could never again organize a counterattack against the Vandals.

v.t: Emperor Wencheng of Northern Wei dies; Emperor Xianwen (Tuoba Hong) ascends
v.s: Emperor Wencheng of Northern Wei died of illness, and his twelve-year-old crown prince Tuoba Hong ascended the throne, known as Emperor Xianwen. Because the emperor was young, Empress Dowager Feng acted as regent. Feng was a capable politician who effectively ruled Northern Wei for over twenty years, implementing a series of Sinicization reforms that laid the groundwork for later Emperor Xiaowen's reforms.

v.t: Frankish king Clovis I defeats Visigoths at Soissons
v.s: Clovis I defeated a Visigothic force at Soissons (modern Soissons, France), pushing the Frankish border south to the Loire River. The conflict between Franks and Visigoths intensified, setting the stage for Clovis's later conquest of the Visigothic kingdom.

d.u: Emperor Ming of Song, Liu Yu

v.t: Liu Song loses all four Huabei provinces, retreats to Huai River
v.s: The Northern Wei general Murong Baiyao captured the last major Liu Song stronghold north of the Huai, Licheng (modern Jinan, Shandong). All four Huabei provinces (Qing, Ji, Xu, Yan) fell into Northern Wei hands. Liu Song's northern defenses retreated to the Huai River line, and it could never recover lost northern territory.

v.t: Eastern Roman Emperor Zeno restored; Basiliscus deposed
v.s: Zeno, having regrouped in Isauria, counterattacked Constantinople. Basiliscus was betrayed and surrendered. Zeno was restored to the throne, and Basiliscus was executed. After regaining power, Zeno faced the dual threats of the Vandals and Ostrogoths.

v.t: Pope and Patriarch of Constantinople continue to quarrel over Christology
v.s: After the Council of Chalcedon, the Pope and the Patriarch of Constantinople still disagreed on the interpretation of Christ's two natures. Pope Leo I insisted on dyophysitism, while the Eastern Church tended toward miaphysitism. The dispute widened the rift between the Eastern and Western churches, sowing seeds for the Great Schism of 1054.

d.u: Emperor Ming of Song, Liu Yu

v.t: Great famine in Nanxuzhou of Liu Song; people suffer terribly
v.s: Due to years of war and flood-drought disasters, the Nanxuzhou area (modern Zhenjiang and Changzhou, Jiangsu) suffered a great famine. Starving people died in the streets; reports even emerged of cannibalism. Emperor Ming of Song, preoccupied with suppressing imperial clan rebellions, showed little concern for relief efforts. The legitimacy of Liu Song rule was increasingly lost.

v.t: Visigothic king Euric ascends, compiles first Germanic written code
v.s: Visigothic king Theodoric II died and was succeeded by his brother Euric. Euric was an outstanding legislator who ordered the compilation of the 'Code of Euric,' the first written code of the Germanic barbarians. The code blended Roman law and Germanic customary law, significantly influencing the medieval European legal system.

v.t: War of the Han River between Goguryeo and Baekje; Baekje defeated
v.s: Goguryeo King Jangsu led his army south to attack Baekje, contending for control of the Han River basin. The Baekje army was routed, and the lower Han River region was occupied by Goguryeo. Baekje's national strength was severely damaged, forcing it to move its capital to Ungjin (modern Gongju, South Korea) and strengthen ties with Silla to resist Goguryeo.

d.u: Emperor Ming of Song, Liu Yu
Emperor Xiaowen of Northern Wei, Yuan Hong

v.t: Emperor Xianwen of Northern Wei abdicates; Emperor Xiaowen Yuan Hong ascends
v.s: Under pressure from Empress Dowager Feng, Emperor Xianwen of Northern Wei (Tuoba Hong) abdicated in favor of his five-year-old crown prince Tuoba Hong (later known as Yuan Hong), who became Emperor Xiaowen. The retired emperor retained some power and tried to oppose Feng. Two years later, he was poisoned by Feng, who then ruled as regent. Xiaowen was raised by Feng and deeply influenced by Han culture, laying the foundation for his later reforms.

v.t: Conflict between Eastern Roman Empire and Ostrogoths; Theodoric defeated in Moesia
v.s: Emperor Zeno sent an army to attack the Ostrogoths in Moesia (modern northern Bulgaria). The Ostrogothic king Theodoric the Great (then still a prince) resisted but was defeated by the Roman forces. Theodoric was forced to move his people westward to Pannonia.

v.t: Emperor Ming of Song dies; Emperor Houfei of Song (Liu Yu) ascends
v.s: Emperor Ming of Song, weakened by long-term alcoholism and debauchery, died of illness in Jiankang. His son Liu Yu ascended the throne, known as Emperor Houfei of Song (Later Deposed Emperor), only eight years old. Ming had entrusted his son to ministers Yuan Can, Chu Yuan, and Liu Mian, but the powerful minister Xiao Daocheng gradually seized real power.

d.u: Emperor Ming of Song, Liu Yu
Emperor Houfei of Song, Liu Yu

v.t: Frankish king Clovis I makes Paris the capital
v.s: Clovis I captured the Île de la Cité on the Seine (the site of modern Notre-Dame de Paris) and moved the Frankish capital from Soissons to Paris. Located in the heart of Gaul with convenient transportation, Paris became the political center of the Frankish Kingdom, a role it has maintained to this day.

v.t: Liu Song minister Xiao Daocheng kills Emperor Houfei of Song, establishes Emperor Shun of Song (Liu Zhun)
v.s: Emperor Houfei of Song, Liu Yu, was dissolute and cruel, so the powerful minister Xiao Daocheng ordered his trusted follower Yang Yufu to kill him. Xiao Daocheng installed Liu Yu's eleven-year-old brother Liu Zhun as emperor, known as Emperor Shun of Song. Xiao Daocheng titled himself Prince of Qi and took full control of the government, with the fall of Liu Song imminent.

v.t: Eastern Roman Emperor Zeno signs treaty with Ostrogoths; Ostrogoths settle in Pannonia
v.s: Zeno signed a treaty with Theodoric the Great, recognizing Ostrogothic rule over Pannonia (modern Hungary) and appointing Theodoric as a military commander of the Eastern Roman Empire. The treaty temporarily relieved the Ostrogothic threat to the Eastern Romans, but the Goths, having gained legal status, became even stronger.

d.u: Emperor Houfei of Song, Liu Yu

v.t: Liu Song prince Liu Xiufan rebels; Xiao Daocheng suppresses
v.s: The Prince of Guiyang, Liu Xiufan, dissatisfied with Xiao Daocheng's domination, raised troops at Jiangzhou (modern Jiujiang, Jiangxi) and marched east toward Jiankang. Xiao Daocheng sent his subordinates Huang Hui and Zhang Jing'er to meet the rebels. Liu Xiufan was defeated and killed. Xiao Daocheng's power was further consolidated for his meritorious suppression of the rebellion.

v.t: Western Roman Emperor Glycerius overthrown by barbarian general Odoacer
v.s: The Western Roman emperor Glycerius came into conflict with the barbarian mercenary leader Odoacer and was overthrown and exiled by him. Odoacer installed Julius Nepos as emperor, but Nepos controlled only a small part of Italy. The Western Roman Empire existed in name only.

v.t: Baekje king Munju assassinated; Samgeun ascends; Wa (Japan) intervenes in Korean Peninsula
v.s: Baekje king Munju was killed in a rebellion, and the young Samgeun ascended the throne, with real power falling into the hands of the minister Hae family. Due to political chaos, Baekje sought aid from Wa (Japan). The Japanese sent troops to the Korean Peninsula to support Baekje against Goguryeo and Silla.

d.u: Emperor Houfei of Song, Liu Yu

v.t: Xiao Daocheng appointed Grand Commandant, takes full control of Liu Song military and civil power
v.s: For suppressing the rebellion of the Prince of Guiyang, Liu Xiufan, Xiao Daocheng was appointed Grand Commandant with command of military affairs in sixteen provinces. Xiao Daocheng now controlled the palace guards in Jiankang and real power in the provinces, accelerating his replacement of Liu Song.

v.t: Eastern Roman Emperor Zeno signs treaty with Vandal king Gundobad
v.s: Unable to recover North Africa, Zeno signed a treaty with Vandal king Gundobad, recognizing Vandal rule over North Africa, Sardinia, Corsica, and Sicily. The Vandal Kingdom thus gained legal independence, no longer recognizing Eastern Roman suzerainty.

v.t: Visigothic king Euric captures southeastern Gaul, reaches the Mediterranean
v.s: Euric captured Arles and Marseille in southeastern Gaul, giving the Visigothic kingdom important ports on the Mediterranean coast. The Visigoths then expanded eastward, controlling the Rhône valley. Euric's expansion made the Visigothic kingdom the most powerful barbarian kingdom in Western Europe.

d.u: Emperor Houfei of Song, Liu Yu

v.t: Liu Song Emperor Houfei becomes tyrannical; court and populace alienated
v.s: At age fifteen, Liu Song Emperor Houfei remained dissolute and cruel, often personally killing for amusement, even breaking into Xiao Daocheng's home to torment Xiao's son Xiao Ze. Xiao Daocheng deeply hated the emperor and began plotting his deposition. The entire court and populace had lost all hope in Houfei's rule.

v.t: Last Western Roman Emperor Romulus Augustulus deposed
v.s: The barbarian mercenary leader Odoacer launched a coup, deposing the fourteen-year-old emperor Romulus Augustulus. Odoacer did not proclaim himself emperor but sent the imperial regalia to Eastern Roman Emperor Zeno, signifying that the West no longer needed an emperor. Zeno granted Odoacer the title 'Patrician of Italy.' The Western Roman Empire, after more than five centuries, formally fell, and Europe entered the Middle Ages.

v.t: Eastern Roman Emperor Zeno orders Ostrogoth Theodoric to invade Italy
v.s: To eliminate the increasingly powerful Ostrogoths, Zeno ordered the Ostrogothic king Theodoric the Great to lead his army into Italy and overthrow Odoacer. Theodoric accepted, leading his Ostrogothic host from Pannonia into Italy, beginning the war to conquer Italy.

d.u: Emperor Houfei of Song, Liu Yu

v.t: Xiao Daocheng kills Liu Yu, seizes control of Jiankang
v.s: The Liu Song minister Xiao Daocheng ordered his confidant to kill Emperor Houfei Liu Yu, and installed Liu Yu's brother Liu Zhun as emperor, known as Emperor Shun of Song. Xiao Daocheng titled himself Prime Minister and Prince of Qi, with the nine bestowments. He fully controlled the Jiankang court, and Liu Song existed only in name.

v.t: Western Roman Empire falls; barbarian chieftain Odoacer rules Italy
v.s: After the barbarian mercenary leader Odoacer deposed the last Western Roman emperor Romulus Augustulus, he became the de facto ruler of Italy. He retained the Roman Senate and administrative system, but real power was in barbarian hands. Eastern Roman Emperor Zeno nominally recognized Odoacer as Patrician of Italy, but the Western Roman Empire no longer existed. The year 476 marks the fall of the Western Roman Empire and the beginning of the Middle Ages in Western Europe.

v.t: Ostrogothic king Theodoric the Great invades Italy
v.s: Theodoric the Great led his Ostrogothic army across the Alps into northern Italy, engaging in fierce battles with Odoacer's forces. The Ostrogoths occupied Milan and Ravenna, and Odoacer retreated to Rome. Theodoric's invasion began the era of Ostrogothic rule in Italy.

d.u: Emperor Shun of Song, Liu Zhun

v.t: Liu Song Inspector of Jingzhou Shen Youzhi raises troops against Xiao Daocheng
v.s: Shen Youzhi (son of Liu Song general Shen Qingzhi), Inspector of Jingzhou, dissatisfied with Xiao Daocheng's domination, raised troops at Jiangling and marched east with forces from Jing and Xiang provinces to attack Xiao Daocheng. Shen Youzhi issued a manifesto condemning Xiao's usurpation and gained support from some provinces. Xiao Daocheng hastily mobilized troops to meet the attack.

v.t: Ostrogothic king Theodoric the Great and Odoacer battle at Verona
v.s: Theodoric the Great defeated Odoacer's army near Verona in northern Italy. Odoacer withdrew to Ravenna with his remaining forces. Theodoric besieged Ravenna but could not take it, and the two sides entered a stalemate. The Ostrogoths controlled most of northern and central Italy.

v.t: Vandal king Gundobad dies; Thrasamund succeeds
v.s: Vandal king Gundobad died and was succeeded by his son Thrasamund. Thrasamund ended the persecution of Catholics that had been carried out by previous Vandal kings and adopted a tolerant religious policy, easing relations with the Eastern Roman Empire.

d.u: Emperor Shun of Song, Liu Zhun

v.t: Xiao Daocheng defeats Shen Youzhi, takes full control of Liu Song
v.s: Xiao Daocheng sent Huang Hui and Xiao Ze to attack Shen Youzhi. Shen Youzhi, defeated near Yingzhou (modern Wuhan, Hubei), committed suicide. Xiao Daocheng had now eliminated the last forces opposing him within Liu Song. He then forced Emperor Shun to abdicate. The fall of Liu Song was imminent.

v.t: Ostrogothic king Theodoric the Great and Odoacer sign treaty to divide Italy
v.s: After a prolonged stalemate, Theodoric the Great and Odoacer reached a treaty: they would rule Italy jointly, with Odoacer in Rome and southern Italy, and Theodoric in Milan and northern Italy. The treaty was a temporary expedient, as Theodoric would soon eliminate Odoacer.

v.t: Zu Chongzhi invents the south-pointing chariot, water-powered trip hammer, and thousand-mile boat
v.s: While serving at the court of the Southern Qi dynasty, the Liu Song scientist Zu Chongzhi, based on ancient records, reconstructed the south-pointing chariot, solving the problem of lost technology. He also invented the water-powered trip hammer (shui dui mo) to improve grain processing efficiency, and built a 'thousand-mile boat' (a fast vessel) that could travel over 100 li a day on the Yangtze. Zu Chongzhi was an outstanding mechanical inventor of ancient China.

d.u: Emperor Gao of Qi, Xiao Daocheng

v.t: Xiao Daocheng usurps the Song throne, establishes Southern Qi
v.s: Xiao Daocheng, the powerful minister of Liu Song, forced Emperor Shun Liu Zhun to abdicate. He proclaimed himself emperor at the southern suburb of Jiankang, with the dynasty name Qi, changing the era name to Jianyuan, and became known as Emperor Gao of Qi. Xiao Daocheng came from a humble background and seized the throne through military achievement and political maneuvering. He drew lessons from Liu Song's downfall, promoted frugality, cleaned up the bureaucracy, and rested the people. The early Southern Qi administration was relatively clean.

v.t: Ostrogothic king Theodoric the Great lures and kills Odoacer, unites Italy
v.s: Theodoric the Great invited Odoacer to a reconciliation banquet in Ravenna, where he personally killed Odoacer. He then executed Odoacer's family and followers, becoming the sole ruler of Italy. He established the Ostrogothic kingdom, with its capital at Ravenna, and titled himself 'King of Italy,' while nominally acknowledging the suzerainty of the Eastern Roman emperor. Theodoric's rule began a period of relative stability and prosperity in Italy.

v.t: Sassanian Persia and Eastern Roman Empire again clash on the border of Asia Minor
v.s: Sassanian king Balash and Eastern Roman emperor Zeno again engaged in military conflict over Armenia in eastern Asia Minor. Both sides had victories and defeats, and eventually the original border was maintained through diplomacy. The Eastern Roman Empire turned its attention to the West, trying to restore influence in Italy.

d.u: Emperor Gao of Qi, Xiao Daocheng

v.t: Northern Wei attacks Southern Qi but is repelled
v.s: Emperor Xiaowen of Northern Wei (at this time young, with Empress Dowager Feng ruling) sent the Xuzhou inspector Xue Huzi to lead a southern campaign against Southern Qi, attacking Qushan (modern Lianyungang, Jiangsu) south of the Huai River. The Southern Qi commander Yuan Chongzu repelled the Wei forces, forcing them to retreat. This failure temporarily caused Northern Wei to abandon southward expansion, and the north-south confrontation remained along the Huai River line.

v.t: Frankish king Clovis I issues the Salic Law
v.s: Based on Frankish tribal customary law, Clovis I compiled and issued the 'Salic Law' (Lex Salica). The code primarily regulated criminal law, civil procedure, and inheritance. The provision that women could not inherit land (the 'Salic law') later served as the legal basis for excluding women from the French royal succession. The Salic Law was the first written code of the Frankish Kingdom and deeply influenced medieval European law.

v.t: Eastern Roman Emperor Zeno dies; Anastasius I ascends
v.s: Zeno died of dysentery without issue. The Senate elected the over-sixty-year-old palace chamberlain Anastasius I as emperor. During his reign, Anastasius carried out fiscal reforms, streamlined the bureaucracy, reduced taxes, restored the Eastern Roman economy, and maintained peace with Persia.

d.u: Emperor Gao of Qi, Xiao Daocheng

v.t: Emperor Gao of Qi dies; Emperor Wu of Qi (Xiao Ze) ascends
v.s: Xiao Daocheng, founding emperor of Southern Qi, died of illness in Jiankang. His crown prince Xiao Ze ascended the throne, known as Emperor Wu of Qi. Emperor Wu continued his father's policies, promoting frugality and cleaning up the bureaucracy, sustaining the prosperity of the 'Yongming Administration.'

v.t: Frankish king Clovis I conquers northern Gaul, defeats last Roman commander Syagrius
v.s: Clovis I defeated the Roman commander Syagrius on the Somme River in northern Gaul. Syagrius surrendered, but Clovis broke his promise and executed him. Clovis thereby took full control of northern Gaul, extending the Frankish kingdom to the English Channel.

v.t: Border conflict between Southern Qi and Northern Wei along the Huai River
v.s: Taking advantage of the death of Emperor Gao of Qi, Northern Wei sent troops to attack Southern Qi's Huai River defenses. Emperor Wu of Qi sent generals Zhou Panlong and Huan Kang to meet the enemy. The two sides confronted each other for months along the Huai, with neither able to achieve a decisive victory. Eventually the Wei army withdrew, and the existing border was maintained.

d.u: Emperor Gao of Qi, Xiao Daocheng
Emperor Wu of Qi, Xiao Ze

v.t: Emperor Wu of Qi ascends; Yongming Administration begins
v.s: Emperor Wu of Qi formally ascended the throne, continuing the governance strategies of his father Xiao Daocheng: reducing taxes, resting the people, and rectifying household registration. Southern Qi enjoyed political stability, economic prosperity, and cultural flourishing, known as the 'Yongming Administration.' The Yongming years were the most prosperous period of the Southern Qi dynasty.

v.t: Ostrogothic king Theodoric the Great forms alliance with Frankish king Clovis I
v.s: Theodoric the Great allied with Clovis I to jointly confront the Visigothic kingdom. Theodoric gave his daughter in marriage to Clovis, making them in-laws. The alliance ensured that the Ostrogoths would remain neutral when Clovis later attacked the Visigoths.

v.t: Burgundian king Gundobad issues the Code of Gundobad
v.s: Gundobad, king of the Burgundians, issued the first written code of the Burgundian kingdom, the 'Code of Gundobad' (Lex Gundobada). The code applied mainly to Burgundians, while a separate 'Burgundian Roman Code' (Lex Romana Burgundionum) applied to Romans. Both codes blended Roman law and Germanic customary law and are valuable sources for the study of early Germanic law.

d.u: Emperor Wu of Qi, Xiao Ze

v.t: Southern Qi promotes 'Yongming style' poetry, advancing literature
v.s: During the reign of Emperor Wu of Southern Qi, the scholars Shen Yue, Xie Tiao, Wang Rong, and others created the 'Yongming style' of poetry, which emphasized tonal patterns and parallelism, paying attention to the 'four tones and eight defects.' This was the beginning of Chinese regulated verse. The Yongming style served as a bridge from earlier poetry to the Tang dynasty's formal verse.

v.t: Eastern Roman Emperor Anastasius I implements monetary reform
v.s: Anastasius I reformed the currency, issuing the high-purity copper coin 'follis' and establishing the exchange rate of 40 folles to one gold solidus. The new monetary system stabilized prices and promoted trade, greatly increasing Eastern Roman revenue. Anastasius's monetary reforms provided financial support for the military campaigns of Justinian the Great.

v.t: Silla king Naemul dies; King Jabi ascends
v.s: King Naemul of Silla died and was succeeded by his son, King Jabi. During Jabi's reign, Silla's relations with Goguryeo and Baekje remained tense. He strengthened diplomatic ties with Southern Qi, seeking to counterbalance Goguryeo and Baekje with help from the Southern Dynasties.

d.u: Emperor Wu of Qi, Xiao Ze
Emperor Xiaowen of Northern Wei, Yuan Hong

v.t: Empress Dowager Feng of Northern Wei issues salary system, reorganizes officialdom
v.s: To curb official corruption, Empress Dowager Feng of Northern Wei issued a 'salary system,' stipulating that local officials receive salaries according to rank and prohibiting arbitrary taxation. Any official embezzling as much as a bolt of cloth was to be executed. The salary system greatly improved Northern Wei's civil service but also sharpened conflicts between Xianbei nobles and Han Chinese scholar-officials.

v.t: Eastern Roman Empire and Sassanian Persia sign Hundred Years' Peace
v.s: After long negotiations, Eastern Roman Emperor Anastasius I and Sassanian king Kavadh I signed a peace treaty for one hundred years. The Eastern Roman Empire would pay Persia a certain amount of gold annually, in exchange for Persia ceasing harassment of the Eastern Roman border. The treaty allowed the Eastern Roman Empire to focus on Western affairs.

v.t: Frankish king Clovis I conquers central Gaul, defeats Visigoths at Tours
v.s: Clovis I led his army south to attack the Visigothic-controlled central Gaul. He defeated a Visigothic army near Tours, capturing the cities of Tours and Poitiers. The conflict between the Frankish and Visigothic kingdoms intensified.

d.u: Emperor Wu of Qi, Xiao Ze
Emperor Xiaowen of Northern Wei, Yuan Hong

v.t: Northern Wei implements equal-field system and three-elders system, deepening Sinicization reforms
v.s: Empress Dowager Feng of Northern Wei, adopting the advice of Li Anshi, issued the 'equal-field edict,' distributing state-controlled land to peasants according to population and collecting rents and taxes. At the same time, she implemented the 'three-elders system,' establishing village, neighborhood, and communal elders to replace tribal chieftains, strengthening grassroots control. These reforms greatly promoted the Sinicization and socioeconomic development of Northern Wei.

v.t: Tang Yu rebellion breaks out in Southern Qi, shakes Jiangnan
v.s: Tang Yu from Fuchun (modern Hangzhou, Zhejiang), dissatisfied with Southern Qi's household registration and taxation policies, led a popular uprising. The rebels captured Tonglu, Qiantang, and other places, with Tang Yu proclaiming himself emperor. The rebel forces grew to some 30,000 and controlled most of Zhejiang. Emperor Wu of Qi sent a large army to suppress the rebellion; Tang Yu was defeated and killed. This rebellion reflected the sharp social contradictions in Southern Qi.

v.t: Eastern Roman Emperor Anastasius I builds the 'Anastasian Wall'
v.s: Anastasius I ordered the construction of a long wall across the Thracian peninsula, running sixty-five kilometers west of Constantinople from the Black Sea to the Sea of Marmara. The wall was 5 meters high and 56 kilometers long, with dozens of fortresses, intended to defend against Huns, Bulgars, and Slavs. This long wall was one of the most impressive defensive works of the ancient world.

d.u: Emperor Wu of Qi, Xiao Ze

v.t: Frankish king Clovis I defeats Roman commander Syagrius at Soissons
v.s: Clovis I defeated the Roman commander Syagrius at Soissons in Gaul. Syagrius fled to the Visigothic kingdom. Clovis occupied the wide area from the Paris basin to the Somme. Roman rule in northern Gaul ended completely, and the Frankish kingdom became the most powerful barbarian kingdom in Gaul.

v.t: Southern Qi and Northern Wei clash at Yiyang; both gain victories
v.s: Clashes broke out between Southern Qi and Northern Wei at Yiyang (modern Xinyang, Henan). The Southern Qi general Chen Xianda defeated a Wei force, recovering some lost territory. Northern Wei quickly counterattacked, and the two sides entered a stalemate at Yiyang, with neither able to achieve a decisive victory.

v.t: King Dongseong of Baekje ascends; rebuilds capital Ungjin
v.s: King Dongseong of Baekje ascended and devoted himself to rebuilding the national strength that had been damaged by Goguryeo. He moved the capital from Ungjin (modern Gongju) to Sabi (modern Buyeo). Dongseong strengthened diplomatic ties with the Southern Dynasties of China and the Wa state of Japan, seeking allies against Goguryeo.

d.u: Emperor Wu of Qi, Xiao Ze

v.t: Southern Qi general Yuan Chongzu defeats Northern Wei army at Qushan
v.s: Yuan Chongzu, Inspector of Qing and Ji provinces for Southern Qi, defeated a Northern Wei southern campaign army at Qushan (modern Lianyungang, Jiangsu). Using terrain and fire tactics, he repelled a Wei force several times his size, killing many. This battle defended Southern Qi's eastern coastal defense line and consolidated Emperor Wu's rule.

v.t: Frankish king Clovis I conquers the Alemanni, expands to the Rhine
v.s: Clovis I led his army east, defeating the Alemanni tribes along the upper Rhine and incorporating most of their territory into the Frankish kingdom. Clovis's power extended to the east bank of the Rhine, making the Frankish kingdom the most powerful barbarian kingdom in Western Europe.

v.t: Fan Zhen publishes 'On the Extinction of the Soul,' triggering a major court debate
v.s: The Southern Qi thinker Fan Zhen published 'On the Extinction of the Soul,' advancing the atheistic view that 'the body and the soul are the same; as long as the body exists, the soul exists; when the body dies, the soul perishes.' This openly denied Buddhist doctrines of karma and reincarnation and the immortality of the soul. His view was strongly opposed by the devout Buddhist Emperor Wu of Qi and the Prince of Jingling, Xiao Ziliang, leading to the largest debate between 'spirit extinction' and 'spirit non-extinction' in the Southern Dynasties.

d.u: Emperor Wu of Qi, Xiao Ze

v.t: Frankish king Clovis I goes to war with the Burgundian Kingdom
v.s: Using the pretext that the Burgundian king Gundobad had killed his brother, Clovis I led his army to attack the Burgundian kingdom. He besieged the Burgundian capital Lyon but could not take it, and the two sides agreed to a truce. Clovis married Gundobad's niece Clotilde, laying the groundwork for the eventual Frankish absorption of Burgundy.

v.t: Prince Xiao Jian of Shixing goes to Yizhou to suppress Liao people's rebellion
v.s: The Liao people (an ancient ethnic group in the southwest) in Southern Qi's Yizhou, unable to bear oppression, launched a rebellion, capturing several counties. Emperor Wu of Qi sent the Prince of Shixing, Xiao Jian, to suppress the rebellion. Xiao used a combination of appeasement and military force to pacify the Liao, consolidating Southern Qi's control over the southwest.

v.t: Ostrogothic king Theodoric the Great promotes Roman law in Italy
v.s: Theodoric the Great promoted Roman law in Italy, preserving the Roman administrative system and legal framework, while also issuing the 'Edict of Theodoric' to coordinate legal application between Goths and Romans. Theodoric's policy of respecting Roman culture facilitated the integration of Goths and Romans and maintained stability in Italian society.

d.u: Emperor Wu of Qi, Xiao Ze

v.t: Ostrogothic king Theodoric the Great conquers southern Italy
v.s: Theodoric the Great led his army south, conquering Roman and Byzantine remnants in southern Italy. He entered Rome and was greeted by the Senate. Theodoric now controlled the entire Italian peninsula, establishing a stable Ostrogothic kingdom.

v.t: Southern Qi and Northern Wei confront each other at Shouyang
v.s: Southern Qi and Northern Wei faced off at the strategic Huai River city of Shouyang (modern Shou County, Anhui). The two sides fought fiercely, with heavy casualties. Emperor Wu of Qi personally went to the front to supervise the troops, eventually forcing Northern Wei to withdraw. This battle preserved the Southern Qi Huai River defense line.

v.t: Hundred Years' Peace between Persia and Eastern Roman Empire expires; border frictions resume
v.s: After the hundred-year peace treaty between the Sassanian Persian king Kavadh I and the Eastern Roman Empire expired, the two sides clashed again over control of the Caucasus. Kavadh reinforced the border, and Anastasius I also strengthened the Caucasus defenses. War seemed imminent.

d.u: Emperor Wu of Qi, Xiao Ze
Emperor Xiaowen of Northern Wei, Yuan Hong

v.t: Empress Dowager Feng of Northern Wei dies; Emperor Xiaowen begins personal rule
v.s: Empress Dowager Feng died in Pingcheng, and Emperor Xiaowen of Northern Wei (Yuan Hong) began to rule personally. Feng had been the architect of Northern Wei's Sinicization reforms, having implemented the salary system, equal-field system, and three-elders system. Xiaowen continued Feng's reform agenda but pushed it further, moving the capital to Luoyang and implementing comprehensive Sinicization of the Xianbei.

v.t: Eastern Roman Empire and Persia reach new treaty, maintaining status quo in the Caucasus
v.s: Anastasius I and Kavadh I reached a new treaty through negotiation. The Eastern Roman Empire agreed to pay Persia an annual sum of gold in exchange for Persia ceasing harassment in the Caucasus. The two sides maintained border stability.

v.t: Indian mathematician Aryabhata publishes the Aryabhatiya
v.s: The great mathematician Aryabhata of the Gupta period published the 'Aryabhatiya,' which systematically summarized Indian mathematics and astronomy. The work introduced the concept of trigonometric functions, calculated an approximation of π (3.1416), and expounded the theory of the Earth's rotation. Aryabhata's work laid the foundation for the golden age of Indian mathematics.

d.u: Emperor Wu of Qi, Xiao Ze
Emperor Xiaowen of Northern Wei, Yuan Hong

v.t: Emperor Xiaowen of Northern Wei reforms official system, promotes Sinicization
v.s: After taking personal rule, Xiaowen reformed the official system, abolishing old Xianbei practices and setting up a Han-style system of three dukes, nine ministers, and departments. He also ordered the use of Chinese (instead of Xianbei) at court, and required Xianbei nobles to dress in Han clothing and adopt Han surnames. Xiaowen's reforms were strongly opposed by conservative Xianbei nobles.

v.t: Emperor Wu of Qi dies; Xiao Zhaoye ascends
v.s: In the tenth year of Yongming, Emperor Wu of Qi died of illness. His grandson Xiao Zhaoye (son of the deceased Crown Prince Xiao Zhangmao) succeeded as emperor, known as the Deposed Emperor of Qi (Yulin King). Xiao Zhaoye was dissolute, ignored state affairs, and trusted sycophants, causing the Southern Qi political situation to rapidly deteriorate.

v.t: Eastern Roman Emperor Anastasius I maintains peace with Ostrogothic king Theodoric the Great
v.s: Anastasius I recognized Theodoric the Great's rule over Italy and granted him the title of consul. Theodoric, in turn, recognized the nominal suzerainty of the Eastern Roman emperor. Both sides maintained peaceful relations. This policy allowed the Eastern Roman Empire to focus on countering Persia and barbarian invasions along the Danube.

d.u: Emperor Wu of Qi, Xiao Ze

v.t: Southern Qi Deposed Emperor Xiao Zhaoye deposed by Xiao Luan; Xiao Zhaowen ascends
v.s: Xiao Luan, a member of the Southern Qi imperial clan and Marquis of Xichang, launched a coup, deposing and executing the dissolute Deposed Emperor Xiao Zhaoye. Xiao Luan installed Xiao Zhaoye's fifteen-year-old younger brother Xiao Zhaowen as emperor, known as Emperor Gong of Qi. Xiao Luan titled himself General of Agile Cavalry and Recorder of Affairs, taking full control of the government.

v.t: Emperor Xiaowen of Northern Wei moves capital to Luoyang, fully implements Sinicization
v.s: Using the pretext of a southern campaign against Southern Qi, Xiaowen led the court and army to Luoyang, formally moving the Northern Wei capital from Pingcheng (modern Datong, Shanxi) to Luoyang. After the move, Xiaowen fully implemented Sinicization policies: changing Xianbei compound surnames to single-character Han surnames (Tuoba changed to Yuan), banning barbarian clothing, adopting Han attire, banning the Xianbei language, encouraging intermarriage between Xianbei nobles and Han scholar-officials, and requiring burial in Luoyang. Xiaowen's reforms were the largest-scale Sinicization movement of a minority group in Chinese history.

v.t: Ostrogothic king Theodoric the Great suppresses a Roman aristocratic rebellion in Italy
v.s: A group of Roman aristocrats conspired to overthrow the rule of Theodoric the Great. Theodoric learned of the conspiracy, quickly suppressed it, and executed the leading Roman senators. After this, Theodoric became more wary of Roman aristocrats but generally maintained cooperation between Goths and Romans.

d.u: Deposed Emperor of Qi, Xiao Zhaoye
Emperor Xiaowen of Northern Wei, Yuan Hong

v.t: Xiao Luan deposes Emperor Gong of Qi, proclaims himself emperor
v.s: Xiao Luan deposed Emperor Gong Xiao Zhaowen and proclaimed himself emperor, known as Emperor Ming of Qi. Xiao Luan massacred the descendants of Emperor Gao and Emperor Wu of Qi, nearly wiping out the Southern Qi imperial clan. During his reign, Emperor Ming was suspicious and paranoid, and Southern Qi politics became dark.

v.t: Ostrogothic king Theodoric the Great and Frankish king Clovis I join forces to defeat the Burgundians
v.s: Theodoric the Great and Clovis I jointly attacked the Burgundian kingdom. Burgundian king Gundobad was defeated, forced to cede territory and submit as a vassal. The Franks and Ostrogoths respectively absorbed northern and southern Burgundian territories, crippling the Burgundian kingdom.

v.t: Visigothic king Alaric II ascends; relations with Franks tense
v.s: Visigothic king Euric died and was succeeded by his son Alaric II. Upon ascending, Alaric II faced a serious threat from the Frankish king Clovis I. He tried to ally with the Ostrogothic kingdom and the Eastern Roman Empire to counter Frankish expansion.

d.u: Emperor Ming of Qi, Xiao Luan
Emperor Xiaowen of Northern Wei, Yuan Hong

v.t: Emperor Xiaowen of Northern Wei announces completion of capital relocation to Luoyang, fully implements Sinicization
v.s: Xiaowen formally announced the completion of the capital relocation to Luoyang and ordered all Xianbei nobles to settle there. He then issued a series of Sinicization decrees: banning barbarian clothing and adopting Han attire; banning the Xianbei language and making Chinese the official language; changing Xianbei compound surnames to Han single-character surnames (Tuoba to Yuan, Dugu to Liu, Bulugu to Lu, etc.); encouraging intermarriage between Xianbei nobles and Han scholar-officials, decreeing that imperial princesses and Xianbei noble daughters must marry Han scholar-officials. These policies greatly accelerated the feudalization of the Xianbei but also provoked rebellion from conservative Xianbei nobles.

v.t: Emperor Ming of Qi massacres imperial clan to secure throne
v.s: To consolidate his rule, Emperor Ming of Qi massively massacred the descendants of Emperor Gao and Emperor Wu of Qi, including the princes of Poyang Xiao Qiao, Suijun Xiao Zilong, and Jian'an Xiao Zizhen, numbering over ten. The Southern Qi imperial clan was nearly wiped out, and Ming's brutality alienated popular sentiment.

v.t: Sassanian king Kavadh I deposed by nobles, exiled to Hephthalites
v.s: Due to his policy of religious tolerance, which offended Zoroastrian priests and nobles, Kavadh I was deposed and exiled to the Hephthalite khaganate. His brother Jamasp temporarily assumed the Persian throne, and the state fell into chaos.

d.u: Emperor Ming of Qi, Xiao Luan
Emperor Xiaowen of Northern Wei, Yuan Hong

v.t: Emperor Xiaowen orders Xianbei surnames changed to Han surnames, inscribed on steles
v.s: Xiaowen issued an edict changing 118 Xianbei tribal compound surnames to Han-style single-character surnames. The imperial clan Tuoba was changed to Yuan; Qiumuling to Mu; Bulugu to Lu; Helai to He, etc. Xiaowen also decreed that Xianbei nobles must be buried in Luoyang, not in Dai (north), and forbade the use of Xianbei surnames in epitaphs. These measures fundamentally transformed the ethnic identity of the Northern Wei upper class.

v.t: Large-scale excavation of Longmen Grottoes begins
v.s: After moving the capital to Luoyang, Emperor Xiaowen of Northern Wei ordered the excavation of grottoes at Longmen Mountain south of Luoyang, which became the Longmen Grottoes. The Guyang Cave, the earliest large cave at Longmen, was built by Xiaowen to pray for Empress Dowager Feng. The inscriptions on the statues in the cave, including the 'Statue-making Inscription by Nun Hui Gan,' are part of the 'Twenty Calligraphic Masterpieces of Longmen,' representing the highest achievement of Northern Wei regular script. Longmen Grottoes, together with Yungang Grottoes and Mogao Caves at Dunhuang, are known as the three major grottoes in China.

v.t: Eastern Roman Emperor Anastasius I builds the 'Anatolian Wall'
v.s: Anastasius I ordered the construction of a defensive wall across Asia Minor from the Black Sea to the Mediterranean, to defend against Persian and Arab invasions. This wall, about fifty kilometers long with dozens of fortresses, was the most important fortification on the eastern frontier of the Byzantine Empire.

d.u: Emperor Ming of Qi, Xiao Luan
Emperor Xiaowen of Northern Wei, Yuan Hong

v.t: Frankish king Clovis I converts to Christianity
v.s: After his victory over the Alemanni, Clovis I, at the urging of his wife, was baptized as a Catholic at Rheims Cathedral, becoming the first Germanic barbarian king to convert to Roman Christianity. Three thousand Frankish warriors were baptized with him. Clovis's conversion won him the support of the Gallo-Roman church and Roman aristocrats, providing the political and religious foundation for the Frankish kingdom's unification of Gaul.

v.t: Northern Wei crown prince Yuan Xun opposes Sinicization, is deposed and killed
v.s: The crown prince of Northern Wei, Yuan Xun (son of Xiaowen), refused to learn Chinese and Han culture, and attempted to flee back to Pingcheng. Emperor Xiaowen was furious: he deposed Yuan Xun, reduced him to commoner status, and soon had him killed. Xiaowen designated his second son Yuan Ke (later Emperor Xuanwu) as the new crown prince. This event demonstrated Xiaowen's determination to enforce Sinicization.

v.t: Kavadh I of Persia restored with Hephthalite support
v.s: The deposed Sassanian king Kavadh I, with the support of the Hephthalite khaganate, led an army back to Persia, defeated his brother Jamasp, and was restored to the throne. After his restoration, Kavadh strengthened centralization and remained dependent on the Hephthalites, but soon began plotting to break free of their control.

d.u: Emperor Ming of Qi, Xiao Luan
Emperor Xiaowen of Northern Wei, Yuan Hong

v.t: Emperor Xiaowen of Northern Wei launches southern campaign, captures Nanyang and Xinye
v.s: Xiaowen personally led a large army south to attack Southern Qi's Nanyang and Xinye (in modern Henan). The Wei army besieged the cities for months, capturing Nanyang, Xinye, and other places; Southern Qi commanders either died in battle or surrendered. Xiaowen's southern campaign expanded the Wei domain southward, bringing it closer to the Yangtze River.

v.t: Emperor Ming of Qi dies; Xiao Baojuan ascends
v.s: Emperor Ming of Qi died of illness in Jiankang, and his crown prince Xiao Baojuan ascended the throne, known as the Marquis of Donghun of Qi. The Marquis was dissolute and cruel, ignored state affairs, favored sycophants, and massacred his ministers, leading Southern Qi to rapid collapse.

v.t: Ostrogothic king Theodoric the Great arranges marriage alliance with Vandal kingdom
v.s: To strengthen relations with the Vandal kingdom, Theodoric the Great married his sister Amalafrida to Vandal king Thrasamund. The two kingdoms formed an alliance to jointly confront the Eastern Roman Empire's influence in Africa. This marriage bond strengthened cooperation among the Germanic kingdoms.

d.u: Emperor Ming of Qi, Xiao Luan
Emperor Xiaowen of Northern Wei, Yuan Hong

v.t: Emperor Xiaowen of Northern Wei again campaigns south, attacks Yiyang
v.s: Xiaowen again led a large army south to attack the Southern Qi city of Yiyang (modern Xinyang, Henan). The Wei army besieged Yiyang for months; the Southern Qi commander Wang Guangzhi held out. Eventually, due to Xiaowen's serious illness, the Wei army withdrew. Though Xiaowen's southern campaign achieved some victories, it failed to break through the Southern Qi Huai River defense line.

v.t: Marquis of Donghun of Qi executes many ministers; political chaos ensues
v.s: After ascending the throne, the Marquis of Donghun of Qi (Xiao Baojuan) was suspicious and cruel. He executed several high ministers, including the Prefect of the Masters of Writing Xiao Yi, the Minister of Works Xu Xiaoci, and General Shen Wenji. He also listened to sycophants Ru Fazhen and Mei Chong'er, dismissing able officials. The Southern Qi court descended into darkness, losing popular support.

v.t: Eastern Roman Emperor Anastasius I and Persia sign new treaty
v.s: Anastasius I and Sassanian king Kavadh I negotiated and signed a new peace treaty, maintaining the status quo in the Caucasus. The Eastern Roman Empire agreed to pay an annual sum of gold to Persia, and Persia promised to prevent Saracen tribes from raiding the Eastern Roman border.

d.u: Marquis of Donghun of Qi, Xiao Baojuan
Emperor Xiaowen of Northern Wei, Yuan Hong

v.t: Emperor Xiaowen of Northern Wei dies; Emperor Xuanwu (Yuan Ke) ascends
v.s: Emperor Xiaowen of Northern Wei died of illness at Guyuanyuan (modern Dengzhou, Henan) during his southern campaign, at age 33. His crown prince Yuan Ke succeeded, known as Emperor Xuanwu. Xiaowen was one of China's most outstanding reformers. His Sinicization policies greatly transformed Northern Wei society but also sowed seeds of conflict between Xianbei nobles and Han scholar-officials. After ascending, Xuanwu partially restored old Xianbei customs, leading to some reversal of Sinicization.

v.t: Marquis of Donghun of Qi becomes even more dissolute, kills loyal ministers
v.s: The Marquis of Donghun of Qi ignored state affairs, spent his days in hunting and play, favored his consort Pan, and extravagantly built palaces. He successively killed the Vice Director of the Imperial Secretariat Xiao Tanzhi, General Cao Hu, and the Vice Director of the Imperial Secretariat Wang Yan. Court officials lived in fear. Regional inspectors became disaffected, and the Southern Qi regime faced crises on all sides.

v.t: Slavs begin large-scale migration into the Balkans
v.s: Slavic tribes (Antes and Sclaveni) began crossing the Danube in large numbers and settling in the Balkan peninsula of the Eastern Roman Empire. They occupied vast areas south of the Danube, infiltrating Thrace, Macedonia, and Greece. Emperor Anastasius I was unable to prevent the Slavic migration and could only reinforce the Danube defenses. This migration laid the foundation for the later formation of the South Slavic peoples.

d.u: Marquis of Donghun of Qi, Xiao Baojuan

v.t: Xiao Yan of Southern Qi raises troops in Yongzhou, campaigns against the Marquis of Donghun
v.s: Xiao Yan (later Emperor Wu of Liang), Inspector of Yongzhou of Southern Qi, after his elder brother Xiao Yi was killed by the Marquis of Donghun, raised troops in Xiangyang, issued a manifesto condemning the Marquis, and marched east toward Jiankang. Xiao Yan gained support from the Prince of Nankang, Xiao Baorong (the eighth son of Emperor Ming of Qi), and several regional inspectors. The curtain rose on the fall of Southern Qi.

v.t: Ostrogothic king Theodoric the Great issues the Edict of Theodoric
v.s: To reconcile legal differences between Goths and Romans, Theodoric the Great issued the 'Edict of Theodoric.' The edict applied mainly to disputes between Goths and Romans, reflecting Roman legal principles while retaining elements of Germanic customary law. This was the most important legal document of the Ostrogothic kingdom.

v.t: Maya city-state of Tikal reaches its peak
v.s: Tikal (modern Guatemala), one of the most important Maya city-states of Mesoamerica, reached its peak. The Tikal king known as 'Curled Nose' (also called Kan Chac) built magnificent pyramid temples, palaces, and ball courts. Tikal became the largest city in the Maya lowlands, controlling extensive trade networks. Maya civilization reached its classic peak during this period.

v.t: Babylonian Talmud completed
v.s: After centuries of discussion and compilation, Jewish rabbis in Babylon completed the 'Babylonian Talmud' (Talmud Bavli). The Talmud is one of the most important texts of Judaism, consisting of the Mishnah and Gemara, covering Jewish law, ethics, customs, historical legends, and more. It became the guide and spiritual support for Jews in the Diaspora.

v.t: Rise of the Ghana Empire: first empire in West Africa
v.s: Approximately 500–1200 CE, the Ghana Empire (located in present-day Mauritania and western Mali) became the first empire in West Africa. Its king was called 'Ghana' (war leader). The empire amassed immense wealth by controlling the West African gold trade and trans-Saharan trade routes. The capital, Koumbi Saleh, was divided into a merchants' city and a royal city, with a population of about 20,000. Arab travelers noted that 'gold was weighed with a scale.' Ghana declined after being conquered by the Almoravids around 1076.

v.t: Rise of the Wari and Tiwanaku empires
v.s: Approximately 500–1000 CE, two large empires emerged in the Andes: the Wari Empire in the highlands of Peru and the Tiwanaku Empire near Lake Titicaca. The Wari were known for their straight road networks and administrative centers such as Pikillaqta; Tiwanaku was famous for its massive stone sculpture 'Gateway of the Sun' and advanced terraced agricultural techniques. Both empires implemented standardized administrative systems in their spheres of influence, laying the foundation for the later Inca Empire. Tiwanaku collapsed around 1000 CE due to climate change.

v.t: Rise of the Moche civilization on the north coast of Peru
v.s: Approximately 100–700 CE, the Moche people on the north coast of Peru established the first state organization in the Andes. They are known for their huge stepped pyramids (such as the Huaca del Sol and Huaca de la Luna), exquisite portrait pottery, and gold work. Moche nobles lived on large platform structures like Huaca del Sol. Moche society was highly stratified, with warrior-priests holding power and practicing human sacrifice. Around 700 CE, the Moche declined due to El Niño events and sand dune encroachment.
